options.explanation: These individuals have additional sex chromosomes e.g., 47 chromosomes (44 .autosome + XXY). questions.text: The mechanism that causes a gene to move from one linkage group to another is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Inversions are a type of variant that can occur in our genomes. They are part of a group of changes known as 'structural rearrangements' that include deletions (where large sections of DNA are missing), repeats (or copy number variants), and translocations within the genome.

Duplication, as related to genomics, refers to a type of mutation in which one or more copies of a DNA segment (which can be as small as a few bases or as large as a major chromosomal region) is produced. Duplications occur in all organisms.

A change in chromosome structure and content caused by translocation is a translocation mutation. Many genes may be transferred between chromosomes. Such translocation mutations can cause disorders of growth, development, and function of the body's cells and systems.

Crossing over is a cellular process that happens during meiosis when chromosomes of the same type are lined up. When two chromosomes — one from the mother and one from the father — line up, parts of the chromosome can be switched. The two chromosomes contain the same genes, but may have different forms of the genes.

questions.explanation: Translocation is a chromosomal abnormality caused by the rearrangement of parts between non­-homologous chromosomes. It may cause a gene to move from one linkage group to another.

questions.text: If a colour­blind man marries a woman who is homozygous for normal color vision, the probability of their son being colour­blind is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The correct option is '0'. If a colourblind man marries a woman who is homozygous for normal colour vision, the probability of their son being colourblind is zero. Colour blindness is a recessive sex-linked trait in which the eye fails to distinguish between red and green colour. A colour-blind man (XcY) and a normal woman (XX) can not have a colour-blind son as it is an X-linked recessive trait and is inherited by criss-cross inheritance. To have a colour-blind son, the mother should be a carrier (XcX) or affected (XcXc) so as to pass the affected X chromosome to the son. Therefore, the correct answer is option A.

questions.text: Which of the following most appropriately describes haemophilia?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chromosomal disorders are due to disjunction occurring during meiosis. In these disorders cells with an abnormal number of chromosomes are present. Hemophilia is a X-linked recessive gene disorder so this option is incorrect.

Dominant gene disorders are due to dominant abnormal genes present on chromosomes. They can be directly transferred to offspring and cause disease in both men and women. Hemophilia is an X-linked recessive gene disorder so this option is incorrect.

Recessive gene disorder is due to recessive genes present on chromosomes. It does not require genes to be dominant. As this option does not clarify that hemophilia is which sex gene recessive disorder this option cannot be regarded as accurate. It is due to X-linked recessive gene disorder.
Haemophilia is a sex-­linked disease. It occurs due to the presence of a recessive sex-linked gene h, which is carried by the X-chromosome. So, this is the correct option.

questions.text: A gene showing co-dominance has:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Alleles that are recessive to each other.
options.explanation: Recessive gene pairs only show recessive traits. Both alleles are dominant and express themselves independently in co-dominance.

options.text: Both alleles independently expressed in the heterozygote.
options.explanation: The phenomenon of expression of both alleles in a heterozygote is called co-dominance. The alleles that do not show a dominance-­recessive relationship and are able to express themselves independently when present together are called codominant alleles. As a result, the heterozygous condition has a phenotype different from either of the homozygous genotypes, e.g., alleles for blood group A (I A) and for blood group B (IB) are codominant so that when they come together in an individual, they produce blood group AB.

options.text: One allele dominant to the other.
options.explanation: When only one gene is dominant it is called complete dominance. Both alleles are dominant and express themselves independently during co-dominance.

options.text: Alleles tightly linked on the same chromosome.
options.explanation: It is the definition of linked genes. This option is irrelevant to co-dominance.

questions.explanation: The phenomenon of expression of both alleles in a heterozygote is called codominance. The alleles that do not show dominance-­recessive relationships and are able to express themselves independently when present together are called codominant alleles. As a result, the heterozygous condition has a phenotype different from either of the homozygous genotypes, e.g., alleles for blood group A (IA) and for blood group B (IB) are codominant so that when they come together in an individual, produce blood group AB.

questions.text: A pleiotropic gene:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Controls a trait only in combination with another gene
options.explanation: It controls multiple traits in an individual.

options.text: Controls multiple traits in an individual
options.explanation: The ability of a gene to have multiple phenotypic effects because it influences a number of characters simultaneously is known as pleiotropy. The gene having a multiple phenotypic effect because of its ability to control expression of two or more characters is called pleiotropic gene. In human beings pleiotropy is exhibited by syndromes called sickle cell anaemia and phenylketonuria.

options.text: Is expressed only in primitive plants
options.explanation: It is expressed in both plants and animals.

options.text: Is a gene evolved during Pliocene
options.explanation: Its evolution is unknown.

questions.text: A man whose father was color blind marries a woman who had a color-blind mother and normal father. What percentage of male children of this couple will be color blind?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the incorrect answer. It is a genetic disorder and is carried by daughter from grandfather to grandson. This diseases is more common in males than females.
This is the incorrect answer. It is a genetic disorder and is carried by daughter from grandfather to grandson. This diseases is more common in males than females.
The child's mother is carrier so she will pass her X chromosome to her sons hence the ratio will be 50 percent
This is the incorrect answer. It is a genetic disorder and is carried by daughter from grandfather to grandson. This diseases is more common in males than females.

questions.explanation: Color blindness is a genetic condition that is inherited in an X-linked recessive manner. The gene responsible for color blindness is located on the X chromosome. In this scenario, let's denote the following: C = Normal color vision c = Color blindness Based on the given information: The man's father is color blind, indicating that the man inherits one affected X chromosome (Xc) from his father. The woman's mother is color blind, indicating that the woman inherits one affected X chromosome (Xc) from her mother and one normal X chromosome (XC) from her father. Now, let's consider the possible genetic combinations for the male children: The man passes his X chromosome to his male children: The man has one Xc chromosome to pass on, as inherited from his father. The woman can contribute either her XC or Xc chromosome. Possible combinations: a) Xc (from the man) + XC (from the woman) = XcXC (normal color vision) b) Xc (from the man) + Xc (from the woman) = XcXc (color blind) Therefore, 50% of the male children will be color blind. It's important to note that this analysis assumes no other genetic factors or mutations influencing color blindness. Additionally, the probability applies to each individual male child, and the actual outcome may vary in practice.

questions.text: Select the incorrect statement with regard to hemophilia:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: They asked the WRONG statement about haemophilia. Haemophilia is a sex-­linked disease which is also known as bleeder’s disease as the patient will continue to bleed even from a minor cut since he or she does not possess the natural phenomenon of blood clotting due to the absence of anti-haemophiliac globulin or factor VIII (hemophilia–A) and plasma thromboplastin factor IX (hemophilia–B, Christmas disease) essential for it. As a result of continuous bleeding, the patient may die of blood loss. It is genetically due to the presence of a recessive sex-linked gene h, carried by the X ­chromosome. A female becomes a hemophiliac only when both of her X­-chromosomes carry the gene (Xh Xh). However, such females generally die before birth because the combination of these two recessive alleles is lethal. A female having only one allele for hemophilia (XXh) appears normal because the allele for normal blood clotting present on the other X­-chromosome is dominant. Such females are known as carriers. In the case of males, a single gene for the defect is able to express itself as the Y­ chromosome is devoid of any corresponding allele (Xh Y).

As a result of continuous bleeding, the patient may die of blood loss. It is genetically due to the presence of a recessive sex-linked gene h, carried by the X ­chromosome. A female becomes a hemophiliac only when both of her X­-chromosomes carry the gene (Xh Xh). In the case of males, a single gene for the defect is able to express itself as the Y­ chromosome is devoid of any corresponding allele (Xh Y).

questions.text: Which of the following cannot be detected in a developing fetus by amniocentesis?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is a chromosomal disorder and, hence can be detected. Screening for Down syndrome can be performed as early as 11 to 14 weeks of pregnancy with a first-trimester ultrasound and blood test. Screening can also be performed between 15 and 20 weeks by a blood test referred to as the multiple marker serum screening test.

Amniocentesis is a fetal sex determination test in which amniotic fluid containing fetal cells that surrounds the developing embryo is extracted and cells are tested for chromosomal patterns to identify genetic disorders, if any. Jaundice is not a chromosomal disorder and thus cannot be tested by amniocentesis.

It is a chromosomal disorder, hence can be detected. Blood or urine samples can reveal abnormal hormone levels that are a sign of Klinefelter syndrome. Chromosome analysis. Also called karyotype analysis, this test is used to confirm a diagnosis of Klinefelter syndrome. A blood sample is sent to the lab to check the shape and number of chromosomes.

It can be detected by observing the karyotype of fetus. The accuracy of determining your baby's gender increases with how far along you are in the pregnancy. The accuracy can vary from 70.3% at 11 weeks to 98.7% at 12 weeks, and 100% at 13 weeks. Eleven weeks is the earliest that sex determination can be carried out with an ultrasound using a method called the 'nub theory'.

questions.text: Down’s syndrome in humans is due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This condition is not related to Down's syndrome. Having three X chromosomes is known as Triple X syndrome (Trisomy X) and can result in its own set of characteristic traits and health issues, but it is distinct from Down's syndrome.

This is the correct explanation. Down's syndrome, also known as Trisomy 21, occurs when an individual has three copies of chromosome 21 instead of the usual two. This extra copy of chromosome 21 leads to the characteristic physical and developmental features of Down's syndrome.

Monosomy refers to the presence of only one copy of a particular chromosome in a diploid organism. Down's syndrome is not associated with monosomy but rather trisomy (the presence of an extra chromosome).
Having two Y chromosomes is known as 47,XYY syndrome, which can result in its own set of characteristics but is unrelated to Down's syndrome.

questions.explanation: Down’s syndrome is the trisomy of the 21st chromosome in men. Down’s syndrome is characterized by short stature, warty skin, protruding tongue, slanting eyes, with folded eyelids. The affected person’s face presents a typical mongoloid look. Hence it is also called mongoloid idiocy. It occurs due to the phenomenon of non­disjunction. Non­-disjunction occurs when a pair of homologous chromosomes do not separate in meiosis but migrate to the same pole of the cell resulting in an uneven number of chromosomes in the daughter cells (45 in one and 47 in the other). This numerical abnormality results in trisomy (2n + 1) and monosomy (2n – 1). Non­disjunction is more common in sex chromosomes.

questions.text: A normal-­visioned man whose father was colour­-blind marries a woman whose father was also colour-­blind. They have their first child as a daughter. What are the chances that this child would be colour­-blind?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: After looking at the crosses, the daughter born to them can be either normal or carrier for the disease, but the possibility of colour blindness is zero percent.

Color blindness, specifically red-green color blindness (which is most common), is linked to a gene located on the X chromosome. In this case: The man is normal-visioned, which means he has one X chromosome with normal vision alleles (let's call it XN) and one Y chromosome (XY). His father was color-blind, which means his father had one X chromosome with color-blind alleles (let's call it Xc) and one Y chromosome (XY). Now, let's look at the woman: She is a carrier of the color-blind allele because her father was color-blind. So, she has one X chromosome with normal vision alleles (XN) and one X chromosome with color-blind alleles (Xc). When they have a daughter, she inherits one X chromosome from each parent: From her father, she inherits the XN chromosome. Her mother, she inherits one of the X chromosomes, which has the XN allele, because carriers of color-blind alleles (Xc) can only pass on the XN allele to their offspring. As a result, the daughter has one XN chromosome and one XN chromosome, which means she has normal vision. Therefore, the chances that this child would be color-blind are 0%.

questions.text: F2 generation in a Mendelian cross showed that both genotypic and phenotypic ratios are the same as 1 : 2 : 1. It represents a case of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Codominance: Codominance results in a 1:2:1 phenotypic ratio, but the genotypic ratio would be different. In codominance, both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygous condition, leading to a 1:2:1 phenotypic ratio, but a 1:2:1 genotypic ratio would not be expected.
Dihybrid cross: A dihybrid cross typically involves two pairs of contrasting traits and would result in a 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio, not the 1:2:1 ratio observed in this case.

Monohybrid cross with complete dominance:In a monohybrid cross with complete dominance, you would expect a 3:1 phenotypic ratio (3 dominant: 1 recessive) in the F2 generation, which is different from the 1:2:1 ratio observed here.

The inheritance of flower color in the dog flower (snapdragon or Antirrhinum sp.) is a good example that shows incomplete dominance. In a cross between true-­breeding red­-flowered (RR) and true­-breeding white­-flowered plants (rr), the F1 (Rr) was pink. When the F1 was self-pollinated, the F2 resulted in the following ratio, 1 (RR) Red: 2 (Rr) Pink: 1 (rr) White. Here the genotype ratios were 1: 2: 1 as in any Mendelian monohybrid cross, but the phenotype ratios had changed from the 3: 1 dominant: recessive ratio to 1 : 2: 1.

questions.explanation: The F2 generation in a Mendelian cross with a 1:2:1 genotypic and phenotypic ratio indicates a monohybrid cross with incomplete dominance. Monohybrid cross with complete dominance: In a monohybrid cross with complete dominance, you would expect a 3:1 phenotypic ratio (3 dominant: 1 recessive) in the F2 generation, which is different from the 1:2:1 ratio observed here. questions.text: A test cross is carried out to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Determine the genotype of a plant at F2
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. A test cross is a genetic cross used to determine the genotype of an individual showing a dominant phenotype. By crossing the individual with a homozygous recessive individual, if all the offspring display the dominant phenotype, it indicates that the tested individual is homozygous dominant. If there is a 1:1 ratio of dominant to recessive phenotypes in the offspring, it suggests that the tested individual is heterozygous.

options.text: Predict whether two traits are linked
options.explanation: A test cross is not typically used to determine whether two traits are linked. Trait linkage is assessed through genetic mapping experiments, such as a dihybrid cross.

options.text: Assess the number of alleles of a gene
options.explanation: A test cross does not provide information about the number of alleles of a gene. It is primarily used to determine the specific alleles an individual carries for a single gene.

options.text: Determine whether two species or varieties will breed successfully or not
options.explanation: A test cross is not used to determine the breeding success of different species or varieties. It is focused on understanding the genetic makeup of an individual for a specific gene.

questions.explanation: A test cross is performed to determine the genotype of the F2 plant. In a typical test cross, an organism showing dominant phenotype and whose genotype is to be determined is crossed with one that is homozygous recessive for the allele being investigated, instead of self­crossing. The progenies of such a cross can easily be analyzed to predict the genotype of the test organism. Given ahead is an illustration of a test cross:

questions.text: When two unrelated individuals or lines are crossed, the performance of F1 hybrid is often superior to both its parents. This phenomenon is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Heterosis, also known as hybrid vigor, is the phenomenon where the F1 hybrid offspring of two unrelated individuals or lines exhibit enhanced traits compared to both of their parents. This is commonly observed in agriculture, where crossbreeding can result in plants or animals with improved characteristics, such as higher yield, better disease resistance, or increased growth rate.

Transformation typically refers to the process by which the genetic makeup of an organism is altered by introducing foreign DNA (often through genetic engineering) to change its characteristics. It is not related to the phenomenon described in the question.

Splicing generally refers to the process of removing introns and joining exons in pre-mRNA to produce mature mRNA during gene expression. It is a fundamental molecular biology process and is not related to the described phenomenon.

Metamorphosis is a biological process that involves a profound transformation in an organism's body structure and form as it progresses through its life stages. This process is particularly well-known in insects like butterflies and amphibians like frogs. It is not related to the enhanced performance of F1 hybrids.

questions.explanation: The increased vigor displayed by the offspring from a cross between genetically different parents is called heterosis. Hybrids from crosses between different crop varieties (F1 hybrids) are often stronger and produce better yields than the original varieties.

questions.text: Which one of the following conditions correctly describes the manner of determining the sex?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Homozygous sex chromosomes (ZZ) determine female sex in birds.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. In birds, heterozygous sex chromosomes (ZW) determine female sex, where females have one Z chromosome and one W chromosome.

options.text: XO type of sex chromosomes determine male sex in grasshopper.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. In some species of grasshoppers, males have only one sex chromosome (XO), while females have two (XX).

options.text: XO condition in humans as found in Turner’s syndrome, determines female sex.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. In humans, females typically have two X chromosomes (XX), while males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY). Turner's syndrome involves a condition where a female has only one X chromosome (XO), which results in female development but with certain medical conditions and characteristics.

options.text: Homozygous sex chromosomes (XX) produce male in Drosophila.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. In Drosophila (fruit flies), males typically have XY sex chromosomes, not XX.

questions.explanation: XO type of sex chromosomes determines the male sex in grasshoppers. This type of sex­-determination comes under the XX­-XO type. Its common examples are cockroaches, grasshoppers, and bugs. The female has two homomorphic sex chromosomes XX and is homogametic. It produces similar eggs, each with an X­-chromosome. The male has one chromosome only and is heterogametic. It produces 2 types of sperms and gymnosperms with X and angiosperms without X. Fertilization of an egg by X­-bearing sperm yields female offspring and by no X sperm yields male offspring.

questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Select the correct statement from the ones given below with respect to the dihybrid cross:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Tightly linked genes on the same chromosomes show higher recombinations
options.explanation: This statement is not correct. Tightly linked genes on the same chromosome show very few recombinations because they are physically close to each other on the chromosome, and crossovers (recombinations) between them are rare.

options.text: Genes far apart on the same chromosome show very few recombinations
options.explanation: This statement is not correct either. Genes that are far apart on the same chromosome can show a higher frequency of recombinations because there is more physical distance between them, increasing the chances of crossovers occurring between them.

options.text: Genes loosely linked on the same chromosome show similar recombinations
options.explanation: This statement is closer to being accurate. Genes that are loosely linked on the same chromosome may exhibit similar recombinations, but the extent of recombinations can vary depending on the distance between them. If they are relatively close, recombinations may still be infrequent.

options.text: Tightly linked genes on the same chromosome show very few recombinations
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Tightly linked genes on the same chromosome are very close together, making crossovers between them rare. As a result, they typically show very few recombinations during a dihybrid cross.

questions.explanation: Linkage is the phenomenon of certain genes staying together during inheritance through generations without any change or separation due to their being present on the same chromosome. Linked genes occur in the same chromosome. The strength of the linkage between two genes is inversely proportional to the distance between the two i.e., two linked genes show a higher frequency of crossing over (recombination) if the distance between them is higher and a lower frequency if the distance is small.

questions.text: Which one of the following cannot be explained on the basis of Mendel’s law of dominance?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The discrete unit controlling a particular character is called a factor.
options.explanation: "The discrete unit controlling a particular character is called a factor." This statement can be explained on the basis of Mendel's law of dominance. Mendel's work identified that genes (factors) are responsible for controlling specific traits, and these factors can exist in dominant or recessive forms.

options.text: Out of one pair of factors one is dominant, and the other recessive.
options.explanation: "Out of one pair of factors, one is dominant, and the other recessive." This statement is the core principle of Mendel's law of dominance. It explains that in a pair of alleles (factors), one allele is dominant and will determine the trait's expression, while the other is recessive and will only be expressed if both alleles in the pair are recessive.

options.text: Alleles do not show any blending and both the characters recover as such in F2 generation.
options.explanation: According to Mendel’s law of dominance, in heterozygous individuals, a character is represented by two contrasting factors called alleles or allelomorphs which occur in pairs. Out of the two contrasting alleles, only one is able to express its effect in the individual. It is called the dominant factor or dominant allele. The other allele which does not show its effect in the heterozygous individual is called recessive factor or recessive allele. Option (c) in the given question cannot be explained on the basis of law of dominance. It can only be explained on the basis of Mendel’s law of independent assortment, according to which in a dihybrid cross, the two alleles of each character assort independently (do not show any blending) of the alleles of other character and separate at the time of gamete formation. Both the characters are recovered as such in the F2 generation producing both parental and new combinations of traits.

Factors occur in pairs." This statement is in line with Mendel's law of segregation, which is closely related to the law of dominance. Mendel observed that factors (alleles) occur in pairs in an individual, and these pairs segregate during gamete formation, with each offspring inheriting one allele from each parent.

questions.explanation: According to Mendel’s law of dominance, in heterozygous individuals, a character is represented by two contrasting factors called alleles or allelomorphs which occur in pairs. Out of the two contrasting alleles, only one is able to express its effect in the individual. It is called the dominant factor or dominant allele. The other allele which does not show its effect in the heterozygous individual is called recessive factor or recessive allele. Option (c) in the given question cannot be explained on the basis of law of dominance. It can only be explained on the basis of Mendel’s law of independent assortment, according to which in a dihybrid cross, the two alleles of each character assort independently (do not show any blending) of the alleles of other character and separate at the time of gamete formation. Both the characters are recovered as such in the F2 generation producing both parental and new combinations of traits.

questions.text: In Antirrhinum, two plants with pink flowers were hybridized. The F1 plants produced red, pink, and white flowers in the proportion of 1 red, 2 pink, and 1 white. What could be the genotype of the two plants used for hybridization? Red flower color is determined by RR, and white by rr genes.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If both plants had the genotype "rrrr" (i.e., homozygous recessive), it would mean that both parents have the genotype for white flowers (rr). In this case, it's highly unlikely that any red or pink flowers would be produced in the F1 generation because there would be no dominant allele (R) for pink or red flowers. Therefore, this option is not consistent with the observed F1 flower colors.

If both plants had the genotype "RR" (i.e., homozygous dominant), it would mean that both parents have the genotype for red flowers (RR). In this case, all offspring in the F1 generation should also have red flowers, and there would be no pink or white flowers. This is not consistent with the observed F1 flower colors, so this option is not correct.

If both plants had the genotype "Rr" (i.e., heterozygous), it would mean that each parent carries one dominant allele (R) for red flowers and one recessive allele (r) for white flowers. This scenario can explain the observed F1 flower colors (1 red, 2 pink, and 1 white). When two heterozygous plants (Rr x Rr) are crossed, they can produce offspring with a ratio of 1 red (RR), 2 pinks (Rr), and 1 white (rr), as seen in the F1 generation. So, this option is consistent with the observed results.

If both plants had the genotype "rr" (i.e., homozygous recessive), it would mean that both parents have the genotype for white flowers (rr). In this case, all offspring in the F1 generation should also have white flowers. This is not consistent with the observed F1 flower colors, so this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The given situation is an example of incomplete dominance where the phenotype found in the F1 generation does not resemble either of the two parents. The genotype of the two plants used for the cross is shown in the figure below. The incomplete dominance of the dominant allele (here ‘R’) over the recessive allele (here ‘r’) could be due to mutations (insertion, deletion, substitution, or inversion of nucleotides). The mutant allele generally produces a faulty or no product. This modification in the product may lead to incomplete dominance of the (unmodified) wild-type dominant allele.

questions.text: Select the incorrect statement from the following:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Galactosemia is an inborn error of metabolism
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Galactosemia is indeed an inborn error of metabolism, where individuals are unable to metabolize galactose properly due to a genetic mutation.

options.text: Small population size results in random genetic drift in a population
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Genetic drift is a phenomenon where the allele frequencies in a small population can change by chance over time. It is more pronounced in smaller populations and can lead to the loss of genetic diversity.

This statement is incorrect. Baldness, or male-pattern baldness, is a sex-influenced trait, not sex-limited. It can affect both males and females, but the pattern and age of onset may differ between the sexes. Hormonal factors, particularly androgens, play a role in its expression.

options.text: Linkage is an exception to the principle of independent assortment in heredity
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Linkage is an exception to the principle of independent assortment. When genes are located on the same chromosome and are physically close to each other, they tend to be inherited together more often because they are less likely to undergo recombination during meiosis.

questions.explanation: Baldness is a sex-influenced trait. The dominance of alleles may differ in heterozygotes of the two sexes. This phenomenon is called “sex-influenced dominance”. Gene products of heterozygotes in the two sexes may be influenced differentially by sex hormones.

questions.text: Sickle ­cell anemia is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Caused by substitution of valine by glutamic acid in the beta globin chain of haemoglobin.
options.explanation: Sickle­-cell anemia is an autosomal hereditary disorder in which erythrocytes become sickle-shaped. It is caused by the formation of abnormal hemoglobin called haemoglobin­-S. Haemoglobin­-S is formed when the 6th amino acid of B-­chain, i.e., glutamic acid is replaced by valine due to substitution. It occurs due to a single nucleotide change (A - T) in the B-globin gene of the coding strand. In the normal B­-globin gene, the DNA sequence is CCTGAGGAG, while in sickle­-cell anemia, the sequence is CCTGTGGAG.

options.text: Caused by a change in a single base pair of DNA.
options.explanation: Sickle­-cell anemia is an autosomal hereditary disorder in which erythrocytes become sickle-shaped. It is caused by the formation of abnormal hemoglobin called haemoglobin­-S. Haemoglobin­-S is formed when the 6th amino acid of B-­chain, i.e., glutamic acid is replaced by valine due to substitution. It occurs due to a single nucleotide change (A - T) in the B-globin gene of the coding strand. In the normal B­-globin gene, the DNA sequence is CCTGAGGAG, while in sickle­-cell anemia, the sequence is CCTGTGGAG.

options.text: Characterized by elongated sickle like RBCs with a nucleus.
options.explanation: Sickle­-cell anemia is an autosomal hereditary disorder in which erythrocytes become sickle-shaped. It is caused by the formation of abnormal hemoglobin called haemoglobin­-S. Haemoglobin­-S is formed when the 6th amino acid of B-­chain, i.e., glutamic acid is replaced by valine due to substitution. It occurs due to a single nucleotide change (A - T) in the B-globin gene of the coding strand. In the normal B­-globin gene, the DNA sequence is CCTGAGGAG, while in sickle­-cell anemia, the sequence is CCTGTGGAG.

Haemoglobin­-S is formed when the 6th amino acid of B-­chain, i.e., glutamic acid is replaced by valine due to substitution. It occurs due to a single nucleotide change (A - T) in the B-globin gene of the coding strand. questions.text: Inheritance of skin color in humans is an example of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Point mutations involve changes in a single nucleotide base pair of DNA and are generally associated with specific genetic disorders or traits caused by a single gene. Skin color, on the other hand, is determined by multiple genes and is not the result of a single point mutation.

Skin color is a classic example of polygenic inheritance. It is controlled by multiple genes, each contributing to the variation in skin color. These genes work together to produce a wide range of skin tones in the human population.

Codominance refers to a situation where both alleles of a gene are expressed fully in the heterozygous condition. While codominance can apply to various traits, it is not the primary mechanism underlying the inheritance of skin color. Skin color is primarily the result of polygenic interactions.
Chromosomal aberrations typically involve structural or numerical changes in chromosomes, leading to genetic disorders. Skin color inheritance does not involve chromosomal aberrations.

questions.explanation: Polygenic (or Quantitative) inheritance is that type of inheritance in which the complete expression of a trait is controlled by two or more genes in which a dominant allele of each gene contributes only a unit fraction of the trait and total phenotypic expression is the sum total of additive or cumulative effect of all the dominant alleles of genes/polygenes. Human skin color is an example of such polygenic inheritance which is controlled by three pairs of polygenes A, B and C. Dark/Black color is due to the presence of all the six dominant contributing alleles AABBCC. Very light color or white color is due to the presence of all six recessive non­contributing alleles: aabbcc.

questions.text: In the hexaploid wheat, the haploid (n) and basic (x) numbers of chromosomes are:

options.explanation: Hexaploid wheat is a result of allopolyploidy induced by doubling the chromosome number of the hybrid produced by crossing two different plants. In hexaploid wheat, Triticale, 2n = 6x = 42. So x stands for basic chromosome number and n for haploid chromosome number. So, n = 21 and x = 7 for hexaploid wheat.

questions.text: Phenotype of an organism is the result of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. The phenotype of an organism is the result of interactions between its genotype (the genetic makeup) and the environment. Genes provide the blueprint for an organism's traits, but how those traits are expressed can be influenced by environmental factors such as nutrition, temperature, and exposure to toxins.

While mutations can undoubtedly lead to changes in an organism's genotype, the phenotype is not solely determined by mutations. Linkages refer to the physical association of genes on a chromosome, which can affect how traits are inherited but are not the primary determinant of an organism's phenotype.

Cytoplasmic effects can influence certain traits, but they are not the primary factors that determine an organism's overall phenotype. Nutrition can affect the development and expression of some traits, particularly in terms of growth and health, but it's only one aspect of the genotype-environment interaction.

Environmental changes can certainly impact the phenotype, but they are not the only factor. Sexual dimorphism refers to differences in phenotype between males and females of a species, which is a specific aspect of phenotype but not the sole determinant of an organism's overall phenotype.

questions.explanation: The phenotype of an organism is the observable physical and behavioural characteristics of an individual. It is influenced by various factors, and these options describe some of those influences: a. Genotype and environment interactions: This option is correct. The phenotype of an organism is the result of interactions between its genotype (the genetic makeup) and the environment. Genes provide the blueprint for an organism's traits, but how those traits are expressed can be influenced by environmental factors such as nutrition, temperature, and exposure to toxins. b. Mutations and linkages: While mutations can undoubtedly lead to changes in an organism's genotype, the phenotype is not solely determined by mutations. Linkages refer to the physical association of genes on a chromosome, which can affect how traits are inherited but are not the primary determinant of an organism's phenotype. c. Cytoplasmic effects and nutrition: Cytoplasmic effects can influence certain traits, but they are not the primary factors that determine an organism's overall phenotype. Nutrition can affect the development and expression of some traits, particularly in terms of growth and health, but it's only one aspect of the genotype-environment interaction. d. Environmental changes and sexual dimorphism: Environmental changes can certainly impact the phenotype, but they are not the only factor. Sexual dimorphism refers to differences in phenotype between males and females of a species, which is a specific aspect of phenotype but not the sole determinant of an organism's overall phenotype. So, the correct answer is (a) Two, as there are only two different kinds of gametes produced by a plant with the genotype AABbCC.

questions.text: Which one of the following is an example of polygenic inheritance?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is an example of polygenic inheritance. Human skin color is determined by several genes that collectively contribute to the variation in skin tones we observe in different populations. Genes control the production of melanin, which is responsible for skin pigmentation.
This is not an example of polygenic inheritance. In many cases, flower color in plants is determined by a single or a small number of genes, leading to distinct color variations.

This is not an example of polygenic inheritance. In honey bees, the sex of an individual is determined by a single gene, with females being diploid and males being haploid. It's a simple Mendelian inheritance pattern.

This is not an example of polygenic inheritance. Mendel's experiments with garden peas, which led to the discovery of the basic principles of genetics, showed that traits like pod shape are controlled by single genes with distinct dominant and recessive alleles.

Let's examine each of the options and determine which one represents an example of polygenic inheritance: a. Skin color in humans: This is an example of polygenic inheritance. Human skin color is determined by several genes that collectively contribute to the variation in skin tones we observe in different populations. Production of male honey bee: This is not an example of polygenic inheritance. In honey bees, the sex of an individual is determined by a single gene, with females being diploid and males being haploid. It's a simple Mendelian inheritance pattern. d. Pod shape in garden pea: This is not an example of polygenic inheritance. Mendel's experiments with garden peas, which led to the discovery of the basic principles of genetics, showed that traits like pod shape are controlled by single genes with distinct dominant and recessive alleles.

questions.text: In order to find out the different types of gametes produced by a pea plant having the genotype AaBb it should be crossed to a plant with the genotype:

The gametes produced by AaBb will be AB, Ab, aB, and ab. However, in this case, the AABB parent will only produce one type of gamete, which is AB. So, this option won't allow us to explore the full range of gametes produced by AaBb.

If you cross AaBb with AaBb, you'll get a mix of genotypes in the offspring, including AaBb, Aabb, aaBb, and aabb. This option will allow us to explore the full range of gametes produced by AaBb because the offspring's genotypes will be diverse.

If you cross AaBb with aabb, all offspring will be heterozygous at both gene loci, resulting in AaBb genotypes. The gametes produced by AaBb will be AB, Ab, aB, and ab. In this case, the aabb parent will only produce one type of gamete, which is ab. So, this option won't allow us to explore the full range of gametes produced by AaBb.

If you cross AaBb with aaBB, all offspring will be heterozygous for the first gene locus (Aa) and homozygous dominant for the second gene locus (BB). The gametes produced by AaBb will be AB, Ab, aB, and ab. The aaBB parent will only produce one type of gamete, which is aB. So, this option won't allow us to explore the full range of gametes produced by AaBb.

questions.explanation: To determine the different types of gametes produced by a pea plant with the genotype AaBb, we need to consider the principle of Mendelian genetics and how alleles segregate during gamete formation. In Mendel's laws, alleles segregate independently, and the different combinations of alleles result in various types of gametes. The genotype AaBb indicates that this pea plant is heterozygous for two different gene loci, with A and a representing one gene and B and b representing another. Let's consider each of the given options for crossing with this genotype and see which one will yield the various types of gametes: a. AABB: If you cross AaBb with AABB, all offspring will be AaBb because all alleles are dominant in the AABB parent, and they will mask the recessive alleles from AaBb. The gametes produced by AaBb will be AB, Ab, aB, and ab. However, in this case, the AABB parent will only produce one type of gamete, which is AB. So, this option won't allow us to explore the full range of gametes produced by AaBb. b. AaBb: If you cross AaBb with AaBb, you'll get a mix of genotypes in the offspring, including AaBb, Aabb, aaBb, and aabb. This option will allow us to explore the full range of gametes produced by AaBb because the offspring's genotypes will be diverse. c. aabb: If you cross AaBb with aabb, all offspring will be heterozygous at both gene loci, resulting in AaBb genotypes. The gametes produced by AaBb will be AB, Ab, aB, and ab. In this case, the aabb parent will only produce one type of gamete, which is ab. So, this option won't allow us to explore the full range of gametes produced by AaBb. d. aaBB: If you cross AaBb with aaBB, all offspring will be heterozygous for the first gene locus (Aa) and homozygous dominant for the second gene locus (BB). The gametes produced by AaBb will be AB, Ab, aB, and ab. The aaBB parent will only produce one type of gamete, which is aB. So, this option won't allow us to explore the full range of gametes produced by AaBb. In summary, option b (AaBb) is the correct choice to find out the different types of gametes produced by a pea plant with the genotype AaBb. When crossed with another AaBb plant, it results in offspring with a variety of genotypes, which allows us to examine all the possible combinations of gametes produced by the AaBb genotype, including AB, Ab, aB, and ab. This demonstrates the principles of Mendelian genetics and allele segregation.

questions.text: G-­6­-P dehydrogenase deficiency is associated with haemolysis of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: G-6-P dehydrogenase deficiency primarily affects red blood cells, not white blood cells (leucocytes). Leucocytes are a type of blood cell involved in the immune system and are not typically affected by this genetic disorder.
Lymphocytes are another type of white blood cell involved in the immune system, and they are not directly affected by G-6-P dehydrogenase deficiency.
Platelets are responsible for blood clotting, and G-6-P dehydrogenase deficiency does not directly affect platelets. Platelet disorders are usually distinct from red blood cell disorders.

This is the correct answer. G-6-P dehydrogenase deficiency is associated with hemolysis (destruction) of red blood cells (RBCs). The deficiency affects the stability of RBCs when they are exposed to oxidative stress, leading to their premature destruction. This condition can result in anemia and other symptoms related to reduced RBC count and function.

questions.explanation: G-6-P (Glucose-6-Phosphate) dehydrogenase deficiency is an inherited genetic disorder that affects the red blood cells (RBCs). This enzyme deficiency can lead to the destruction (hemolysis) of red blood cells under certain conditions, typically triggered by exposure to certain drugs, infections, or foods.

questions.text: A man and a woman, who do not show any apparent signs of a certain inherited disease, have seven children (2 daughters and 5 sons). Three of the sons suffer from the given disease but none of the daughters are affected. Which of the following mode of inheritance do you suggest for this disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This mode of inheritance would mean that if one parent has the disease (in this case, the father or the mother), then all the children, regardless of gender, would have a 50% chance of inheriting the disease. However, the information provided indicates that neither parent shows signs of the disease, which is not consistent with a sex-linked dominant pattern. None of the daughters are affected, which is unusual for a sex-linked dominant condition.

In a sex-linked recessive pattern, the disease is carried on the X chromosome. Males (XY) are more likely to be affected because they inherit a single X chromosome, and if that X chromosome carries the disease allele, they will express the disease. Females (XX) can be carriers if they inherit one normal X and one X with the disease allele, and they do not show signs of the disease. This mode of inheritance is consistent with the information provided in the question, as three out of the five sons suffer from the disease, and none of the daughters are affected.

Sex-limited inheritance is when a trait is expressed in only one sex but not the other, even though both sexes can carry the gene. This option doesn't align with the information provided because the disease appears to be expressed in males and not limited to one sex.

In an autosomal dominant pattern, the disease would typically manifest in individuals who inherit one copy of the dominant disease allele from an affected parent. This option is not consistent with the information provided because both parents do not show signs of the disease, and none of the daughters are affected.

questions.explanation: The most likely mode of inheritance for this disease, based on the given information, is b. Sex-linked recessive. This pattern explains why the sons are affected, and the daughters are carriers without displaying symptoms, while the unaffected parents do not show signs of the disease because they have one normal X chromosome. In summary, the inheritance pattern for the disease in this family appears to be consistent with a sex-linked recessive mode of inheritance. This means that the disease is carried on the X chromosome, leading to its expression in males and its carrier status in females. The information provided does not align with the other inheritance patterns mentioned.

questions.text: A woman with 47 chromosomes due to three copies of chromosome 21 is characterized by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Superfemales refer to individuals with a chromosomal anomaly known as XXX syndrome (trisomy X). They have an extra X chromosome (47,XXX) compared to the typical female karyotype (46,XX). Trisomy X individuals typically do not have an extra chromosome 21, and their condition does not manifest as Down's syndrome. Instead, it can be associated with some developmental and learning differences but generally does not result in the characteristic features of Down's syndrome.

Triploidy refers to having an extra set of all chromosomes, resulting in 69 chromosomes in total (3n). In triploidy, there is no specific mention of an extra chromosome 21, and it doesn't lead to Down's syndrome.

Turner's syndrome is characterized by the absence of one X chromosome in females (45,X or other X monosomies). This condition does not involve an extra chromosome 21 and does not result in Down's syndrome.

Down's syndrome is caused by the presence of an extra copy of chromosome 21 (trisomy 21). Individuals with Down's syndrome have 47 chromosomes instead of the usual 46. The characteristic features of Down's syndrome include intellectual disability, distinctive facial features, and certain health issues, such as heart defects.

questions.explanation: The woman with 47 chromosomes due to three copies of chromosome 21 has Down's syndrome. A woman with 47 chromosomes due to an additional copy of chromosome 21 exhibits the characteristic features of Down's syndrome, which includes intellectual disability and distinctive facial features. The other options, such as superfemales, triploidy, and Turner's syndrome, do not accurately describe the condition resulting from the trisomy of chromosome 21.

questions.text: Haemophilia is more commonly seen in human males than in human females because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A greater proportion of girls die in infancy.
options.explanation: This option does not explain the higher prevalence of hemophilia in males. Hemophilia is a genetic condition, and infant mortality rates do not directly relate to the inheritance pattern of this disease.

options.text: This disease is due to a X-­linked recessive mutation.
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Hemophilia is caused by a mutation in the genes located on the X chromosome. Since males have one X chromosome and one Y chromosome, if their X chromosome carries the mutation, they will express the disease because they lack a second, healthy X chromosome to compensate for it. In contrast, females have two X chromosomes, so they would need two copies of the mutant gene to express the disease, making it less common in females.

options.text: This disease is due to a Y-­linked recessive mutation.
options.explanation: Hemophilia is not due to a Y-linked mutation. Y-linked mutations primarily affect males, but these mutations are generally associated with male-specific traits and don't account for the prevalence of hemophilia in males.

options.text: This disease is due to an X-­linked dominant mutation.
options.explanation: Hemophilia is not caused by an X-linked dominant mutation. In X-linked dominant conditions, the presence of a single affected X chromosome would lead to the expression of the disease in both males and females. This is not the case with hemophilia, where the mutant allele is recessive.

questions.explanation: Hemophilia is more commonly observed in human males than in human females because it is caused by a recessive mutation on the X chromosome. Males, having only one X chromosome, are more likely to express the disease if they inherit the mutant allele, while females need two copies of the mutant allele to manifest the condition. This difference in chromosome composition explains the higher prevalence of hemophilia in males.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a hereditary disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cystic fibrosis is a hereditary disease. It is caused by mutations in the CFTR gene and is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner, meaning that both parents must carry the faulty gene for a child to develop the disease.

Thalassemia is a hereditary disease that affects the production of hemoglobin, a protein in red blood cells. It is caused by mutations in various genes related to hemoglobin production. Thalassemia can be inherited in an autosomal recessive manner, where a person needs to inherit two faulty genes (one from each parent) to develop the disease.

Haemophilia is also a hereditary disease. It is caused by mutations in genes on the X chromosome and is inherited in an X-linked recessive manner. Males are more commonly affected because they have only one X chromosome, while females have two, providing a backup copy of the gene.

Cretinism is not a hereditary disease. It is a condition related to severe congenital hypothyroidism and is typically due to iodine deficiency during pregnancy or early infancy. While iodine deficiency can have environmental and nutritional factors, it is not directly linked to genetic inheritance.

questions.explanation: Cretinism is not a hereditary disease; it is a condition related to severe congenital hypothyroidism, often caused by iodine deficiency during pregnancy or early infancy. On the other hand, cystic fibrosis, thalassemia, and hemophilia are hereditary diseases with genetic causes and patterns of inheritance.

questions.text: A woman with normal vision, but whose father was color blind, marries a color blind man. Suppose that the fourth child of this couple was a boy. This boy:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: May be colour blind or may be of normal vision
options.explanation: This option suggests that the boy's vision is uncertain. It acknowledges that the boy could inherit either a normal vision allele or a color-blind allele from his mother. Since his mother is a carrier (heterozygous), there is a 50% chance that he will inherit the normal vision allele and a 50% chance that he will inherit the color-blind allele. The outcome is probabilistic.

This statement is inaccurate. While the boy has a high probability of being color blind due to his mother's carrier status, it is not a certainty. There is a chance he could inherit the normal vision allele from his father.
This statement is also incorrect. The boy has a high probability of being color blind due to the maternal carrier status, so it is not a guarantee that he will have normal color vision.

options.text: Will be partially colour blind since he is heterozygous for the colour blind mutant allele
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. The term "partially color blind" is not a valid description in this context. In the case of X-linked recessive color blindness, individuals are typically either color blind (hemizygous for the color-blind allele) or have normal color vision.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the boy's vision is uncertain. He may either be color blind or have normal vision, as it depends on which allele he inherits from his mother. This situation is determined by the probabilistic nature of inheritance in X-linked recessive traits, where carriers (heterozygous individuals) have a 50% chance of passing on the mutant allele.

questions.text: In a plant, red fruit (R) is dominant over yellow fruit (r) and tallness (T) is dominant over shortness (t). If a plant with RRTt genotype is crossed with a plant that is rrtt:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: 25% will be tall with red fruit
options.explanation: This corresponds to the genotype RTTT, which is only one out of four possible combinations of alleles in the offspring. Therefore, this option suggests that 25% of the offspring will be tall with red fruit.

options.text: 50% will be tall with red fruit
options.explanation: This is the combination RTTt, which is two out of four possible combinations of alleles. As such, it represents 50% of the offspring. This is the correct answer.

options.text: 75% will be tall with red fruit
options.explanation: This option incorrectly suggests that both RTTT and RTTt combinations together represent 75% of the offspring. This is not the case; the correct percentage is 50%.

options.text: All the offspring will be tall with red fruit
options.explanation: This option is not correct because not all offspring have the genotype RTTT. Only 25% will have this genotype.

questions.explanation: When a plant with the genotype RRTt (red fruit and tall) is crossed with a plant that is rrtt (yellow fruit and short), the Punnett square analysis shows that 50% of the offspring will be tall with red fruit (genotype RTTt). Therefore, the correct answer is option b. The other options either overestimate or underestimate the percentage of offspring with the specified genotype.

questions.text: Lack of independent assortment of two genes A and B in fruit fly Drosophila is due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Repulsion refers to a situation where two alleles for different genes on homologous chromosomes are present in the same organism. This doesn't cause a lack of independent assortment but rather leads to heterozygosity at two different loci on the same chromosome.

Recombination is the process of creating new combinations of alleles in offspring due to crossing over during meiosis. While recombination can affect the distribution of alleles for two genes, it is not the primary cause of the lack of independent assortment between two genes located close together.

Linkage is the correct answer. Genetic linkage occurs when two genes are located near each other on the same chromosome. Because of their physical proximity, they tend to be inherited together more often than not. This linkage can result in deviations from expected Mendelian ratios.

Crossing over is a process during meiosis in which genetic material is exchanged between homologous chromosomes. While crossing over can disrupt genetic linkage and lead to the independent assortment of genes, it typically occurs between genes that are relatively far apart on the same chromosome.

questions.explanation: The lack of independent assortment of two genes A and B in a fruit fly Drosophila is due to genetic linkage (option c). Genetic linkage occurs when two genes are physically close to each other on the same chromosome, causing them to be inherited together more frequently. While recombination (option b) and crossing over (option d) can affect the distribution of alleles for these genes, linkage is the primary reason for their non-independent assortment. Repulsion (option a) doesn't relate to the lack of independent assortment but instead refers to alleles on homologous chromosomes in the same individual.

questions.text: One of the parents of a cross has a mutation in its mitochondria. In that cross, that parent is taken as a male. During segregation of F2 progenies that mutation is found in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not the correct answer because mitochondrial DNA is usually inherited exclusively from the mother in most organisms, and therefore, the mutation from the male parent would not be present in one-third of the progeny.
This is a reasonable possibility. If the male parent with the mitochondrial mutation does not contribute his mitochondria to the offspring, then none of the progeny would inherit the mutation.
This is not typically the case, as mitochondrial DNA is usually inherited from the mother.
This is not the correct answer because mitochondrial DNA is primarily inherited from the mother.

questions.explanation: When one of the parents in a cross has a mutation in its mitochondria and is considered the male, the mutation is usually found in none of the progenies (option b). This is because mitochondrial DNA is typically inherited maternally, and the male's mitochondrial DNA does not contribute to the offspring.

questions.text: The recessive genes located on the X ­chromosome of humans are always:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Recessive genes located on the X chromosome in humans are not always lethal. While some X-linked recessive conditions can be severe and, in some cases, life-threatening (such as hemophilia), many are not lethal and can manifest as various traits or disorders. Therefore, this is not the correct answer.
"Sub-lethal" is not a term commonly used in genetics to describe X-linked recessive genes. This option is not relevant to the context of X-linked recessive genes.

This is the correct answer if the question is specifically referring to a situation where a recessive X-linked gene is expressed in males. However, in classical genetics and most general contexts, recessive X-linked genes are typically expressed in females when homozygous or heterozygous, while in males, they are always expressed due to having only one X chromosome and no Y chromosome to mask the effect.

In classical genetics and most general contexts, recessive genes located on the X chromosome are typically expressed in females when they carry two copies of the recessive allele (homozygous) or when they carry one copy of the recessive allele (heterozygous). In males, these genes are always expressed because they have only one X chromosome and no corresponding allele on the Y chromosome to mask the effect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer depends on the specific context or terminology used. In classical genetics and most general contexts, recessive X-linked genes are typically expressed in females (option d) in certain conditions, while in males, they are always expressed.

questions.text: Which one of the following traits of garden pea studied by Mendel was a recessive feature?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The trait of axial flower position was one of Mendel's studied traits. It is associated with dominant (A) and recessive (a) alleles. Axial (A) is dominant over terminal (a).
The trait of green seed color was also one of Mendel's studied traits. It is associated with a recessive allele (g). Yellow seed color (G) is dominant over green (g).

Green pod color is not typically described as one of Mendel's studied traits in pea plants. Mendel primarily focused on seed color, seed shape, flower color, and other traits. The concept of "green pod color" is not relevant to Mendel's experiments.
The trait of round seed shape (R) was studied by Mendel and is associated with a dominant allele. Wrinkled seed shape (r) is the recessive trait.

questions.explanation: The recessive feature among the provided options that Mendel studied in garden pea plants was the green seed color (option b). Mendel's experiments revealed that green seed color is the result of a recessive allele (g), with yellow seed color being dominant (G).

questions.text: The genes controlling the seven pea characters studied by Mendel are now known to be located on how many different chromosomes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option suggests that the genes controlling the seven pea characters are located on seven different chromosomes. In reality, this is not the case. Organisms typically have fewer chromosomes than the total number of traits they possess, as genes are located on these chromosomes.

This option suggests that the genes controlling the seven pea characters are located on six different chromosomes. While this could be a possibility, it is not accurate in the context of Mendel's experiments and the genetic understanding of garden pea traits.

This option suggests that the genes controlling the seven pea characters are located on five different chromosomes. While it's closer to the correct answer, it still does not accurately represent the genetic basis of these traits in garden peas.

This is the correct answer. The genes controlling the seven pea characters studied by Mendel are now known to be located on four different chromosomes. Mendel's work with garden peas helped lay the foundation for our understanding of genetic inheritance, including the principle of independent assortment, which is based on the behavior of genes on different chromosomes.

questions.explanation: The genes controlling the seven pea characters studied by Mendel are now known to be located on four different chromosomes (option d). This illustrates the concept of independent assortment, where genes on separate chromosomes assort independently during gamete formation, leading to a variety of trait combinations in the offspring.

questions.text: Two crosses between the same pair of genotypes or phenotypes in which the sources of the gametes are reversed in one cross, is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A test cross, also known as a back cross, is a genetic cross used to determine the genotype of an individual with a dominant phenotype. It involves crossing the individual with a known homozygous recessive genotype. The progeny's phenotypes can reveal the genotype of the individual being tested.

A reciprocal cross is a type of genetic cross in which two crosses are performed between the same pair of genotypes or phenotypes, but the sources of the gametes are reversed between the two crosses. For example, if you cross a male with a female in the first cross, you would switch the male and female roles in the second cross. Reciprocal crosses help determine if there is a difference in the inheritance of traits based on the sex of the parent providing the trait.

A dihybrid cross is a genetic cross between two individuals that are heterozygous for two different genes (i.e., they carry two different traits that are controlled by separate genes). This type of cross is used to study the inheritance patterns of two traits simultaneously. It involves the consideration of two pairs of alleles and their combinations in the offspring.
"Reverse cross" is not a standard term in genetics, and it doesn't have a specific meaning in the context of genetic crosses.

questions.explanation: A test cross is used to determine the genotype of an individual with a dominant phenotype by crossing it with a known homozygous recessive individual. A reciprocal cross involves performing two crosses between the same genotypes or phenotypes but reversing the sources of gametes in one of the crosses. This is often used to test for sex-linked inheritance or parental effects. A dihybrid cross is a genetic cross involving two individuals heterozygous for two different genes to study the inheritance of both traits simultaneously. "Reverse cross" is not a recognized term in genetics. In the context of your question, the correct answer is "b. Reciprocal cross." It's an important tool in genetics to explore the effects of parental contributions in different ways and is not related to the other options provided.

questions.text: Pattern baldness, moustaches and beard in human males are examples of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sex-linked traits are those traits that are located on the sex chromosomes, X and Y, and are often associated with genes on the X chromosome. These traits are typically inherited differently in males and females due to the differences in the number of sex chromosomes (XY in males, XX in females). Classic examples of sex-linked traits include hemophilia and color blindness.

Sex-limited traits are those that are only expressed in one sex and not the other. For example, traits related to pregnancy and lactation are sex-limited to females. In males, these traits are not expressed.

Sex-influenced traits are those that are influenced by the sex of an individual but can be expressed in both males and females. The expression of these traits may be different between the sexes due to hormonal or genetic factors. Pattern baldness, mustaches, and beards in human males are influenced by sex hormones, primarily androgens (e.g., testosterone), and they tend to be more prominent in males compared to females.

Sex-determining traits are those that determine an individual's biological sex. These traits are usually related to the presence or absence of specific sex chromosomes. In humans, the presence of an X and a Y chromosome typically results in a male, while the presence of two X chromosomes results in a female.

questions.explanation: Sex-linked traits are located on the sex chromosomes and are inherited differently in males and females. Sex-limited traits are expressed exclusively in one sex and not the other. Sex-influenced traits are influenced by the sex of the individual and can be expressed in both sexes, with differences in expression often due to hormonal or genetic factors. Sex-determining traits determine an individual's biological sex based on the presence or absence of specific sex chromosomes. In the context of your question, pattern baldness, mustaches, and beards in human males are examples of "c. Sex-influenced traits" because they are influenced by sex hormones and can be expressed in both males and females but tend to be more prominent in males due to hormonal factors.

questions.text: Which one of the following conditions though harmful in itself, is also a potential savior from a mosquito-borne infectious disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thalassemia is a genetic blood disorder that affects the production of hemoglobin. It can lead to anemia, fatigue, and other health complications but is not typically associated with protection from mosquito-borne diseases.

Sickle cell anemia is a genetic blood disorder in which red blood cells take on a characteristic sickle shape. While it can be harmful, it provides a degree of protection against malaria, a mosquito-borne infectious disease. Malaria parasites have difficulty infecting and surviving in sickled red blood cells.

Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune condition that affects the body's ability to absorb vitamin B12. It can lead to anemia and other health issues but is not related to protection from mosquito-borne diseases.
Leukemia is a type of cancer that affects the blood and bone marrow. It is not related to protection from mosquito-borne infectious diseases.

questions.explanation: Thalassemia is a genetic blood disorder but is not associated with protection from mosquito-borne diseases. Sickle cell anemia, while harmful in itself, provides some protection against malaria, a mosquito-borne infectious disease. Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune condition but is not related to protection from mosquito-borne diseases. Leukemia is a type of blood cancer and is not associated with protection from mosquito-borne diseases. The correct answer in this context is "b. Sickle cell anemia." This condition can be harmful but offers some protection against malaria, making it a potential savior from this mosquito-borne infectious disease.

questions.text: Down’s syndrome is caused by an extra copy of chromosome number 21. What percentage of offspring produced by an affected mother and a normal father would be affected by this disorder?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option means that all offspring produced by an affected mother and a normal father would be affected by Down syndrome. This is not accurate. In cases of Down syndrome, there's a chance for genetic variability in the offspring due to how the extra chromosome is inherited.

This percentage suggests that three out of four offspring would be affected by Down syndrome. This is not accurate. The inheritance of an extra chromosome 21 does not follow a Mendelian pattern where you would expect this ratio.

This option indicates that half of the offspring would be affected by Down syndrome, and this is the correct answer. It's important to understand that Down syndrome results from the presence of an extra copy of chromosome 21. When an affected mother (with three copies of chromosome 21) has a child with a normal father (with two copies of chromosome 21), there is a 50% chance that each offspring will inherit the extra chromosome from the mother, leading to Down syndrome, and a 50% chance that they will inherit the typical two copies of chromosome 21. This is a simplified explanation as there may be some variations in the genetic transmission.

This percentage suggests that one out of four offspring would be affected by Down syndrome. This is not accurate because, as explained earlier, there is a 50% chance of each offspring inheriting the extra chromosome 21 from the affected mother.

questions.explanation: Down syndrome is caused by an extra copy of chromosome 21 (trisomy 21). When an affected mother (with three copies of chromosome 21) has a child with a normal father (with two copies of chromosome 21), there is a 50% chance that each offspring will inherit the extra chromosome from the mother, leading to Down syndrome, and a 50% chance that they will inherit the typical two copies of chromosome 21. The genetic transmission is not a simple Mendelian pattern, but this 50% probability reflects the likely outcome.

questions.text: In Drosophila, the sex is determined by
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The ratio of number of X­-chromosome to the sets of autosomes
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate for Drosophila or most organisms. Sex determination typically involves specific sex chromosomes and not a ratio of X-chromosomes to autosomes.
This option is not applicable to Drosophila. While X and Y chromosomes determine the sex in some species (like humans), Drosophila use a different mechanism.

options.text: The ratio of pairs of X-­chromosomes to the pairs of autosomes
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. In Drosophila, sex determination is influenced by the ratio of X-chromosomes to autosomes (X/A ratio). Specifically, when the X/A ratio is equal to 0.5 (X/A = 0.5), it results in the development of a male (XY), and when the X/A ratio is equal to 1.0 (X/A = 1.0), it leads to the development of a female (XX).

options.text: Whether the egg is fertilized or develops parthenogenetically
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate for Drosophila. Their sex determination is primarily based on the X/A ratio, and it is not determined by whether the egg is fertilized or develops parthenogenetically.

questions.explanation: In Drosophila, the sex determination mechanism involves the X/A ratio, where a specific ratio of X-chromosomes to autosomes determines the sex of the individual. A 0.5 X/A ratio results in males (XY), and a 1.0 X/A ratio results in females (XX). This mechanism differs from many other species, such as humans, where sex is determined by specific sex chromosomes (X and Y) rather than a ratio.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of pleiotropy?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hemophilia is a genetic disorder that affects blood clotting. It is primarily caused by mutations in genes responsible for blood clotting factors. While it has various effects on bleeding and clotting, it is not typically considered an example of pleiotropy because the gene mutations primarily affect a single physiological process (blood clotting).

Thalassemia is a group of genetic disorders that affect the production of hemoglobin. While different types of thalassemia exist, the primary effect of thalassemia mutations is on the production and structure of hemoglobin and red blood cells. Like hemophilia, it is not a classic example of pleiotropy because the effects are largely related to one physiological process (blood and oxygen transport).

Sickle cell anemia is a genetic disorder caused by a mutation in the hemoglobin gene. This mutation has multiple effects, including changing the shape of red blood cells, leading to problems with blood flow, pain crises, organ damage, and an increased risk of infection. These multiple effects on different physiological processes make it a classic example of pleiotropy.

Color blindness is typically caused by genetic mutations that affect the perception of color due to the presence of photoreceptor proteins in the retina. While it impacts an individual's ability to perceive colors, the effects are primarily limited to the visual system and are not typically considered pleiotropic.

questions.explanation: Pleiotropy refers to the phenomenon where a single gene has multiple effects on an organism's phenotype. Among the options provided, "c. Sickle cell anemia" is an example of pleiotropy because the mutation in the hemoglobin gene affects various physiological processes, leading to a range of symptoms and complications beyond just the structure of hemoglobin

questions.text: There are three genes a, b, c. The percentage of crossing over between a and b is 20%, b and c is 28% and a and c is 8%. What is the sequence of genes on the chromosome?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option suggests that the genes on the chromosome are ordered as follows: b, a, and c. This order implies that gene b is located closest to gene c, with gene a in between. This arrangement is consistent with the given crossing over percentages: 20% between a and b, 28% between b and c, and 8% between a and c.

This option proposes that the genes are ordered as a, b, c. However, this order is not consistent with the provided crossing over percentages. In this arrangement, it suggests a 20% crossover between a and b, which does not match the given data.

This option proposes the order a, c, b. While this order is consistent with the 8% crossing over between a and c, it does not match the 20% crossing over between a and b and the 28% crossing over between b and c.

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: This is not the correct answer, as option a is indeed correct based on the information provided.

questions.explanation: Based on your assertion, option "a. b, a, c" is considered the correct answer. It implies that the genes are ordered on the chromosome as b, a, c. This arrangement aligns with the provided crossing over percentages and is consistent with the data. Please note that gene mapping based on crossing over percentages can be complex, and your interpretation is valid if it corresponds to the given information.

questions.text: A gene is said to be dominant if:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It expresses it’s effect only in homozygous state.
options.explanation: This statement refers to a recessive gene. A recessive gene only expresses its effect when an individual has two copies of the recessive allele (homozygous recessive). In heterozygous individuals, the dominant allele masks the expression of the recessive allele.

options.text: It expresses it’s effect only in heterozygous condition.
options.explanation: This situation doesn't occur in traditional Mendelian genetics. Genes are typically either dominant or recessive, and their effects are not limited to the heterozygous condition.
