questions.text: Two dominant nonallelic genes are 50 map units apart. The linkage is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When both the recessive or the dominant alleles for two traits are on the same chromosome, it is called the cis phase.
When a recessive and dominant allele for the different traits are on the same chromosome, we call it the trans phase.

When genes are closely located in chromosomes and there is no chance of separating them by even crossing over, then it is called complete linkage. They are transmitted together to the same gamete and offspring.

Chromosome mapping is based on two genetic principles: (i) The frequency of crossing over between two genes is directly proportional to the distance between them in the chromosome. (ii) Genes are arranged in a linear order in the chromosome. 50 map unit distance between the genes is quite enough to change the cis arrangement of dominant genes into trans. So, there is no fixed linkage present.

questions.explanation: Two dominant non-­allelic genes are 50 map units apart. The linkage is absent/incomplete. Chromosome mapping is based on the fact that genes are linearly arranged in the chromosome and frequency of crossing over is directly proportional to the distance between two genes. Dominant genes show cis arrangement. At 50 map units cis is changed to trans and vice-­versa hence no fixed linkage is present.

questions.text: A polygenic inheritance in human beings is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A polygenic trait is a characteristic, such as height or skin color, that is influenced by two or more genes. Because multiple genes are involved, polygenic traits do not follow the patterns of Mendelian inheritance. Many polygenic traits are also influenced by the environment and are called multifactorial. Polygenic (or Quantitative) inheritance is that type of inheritance in which the Complete expression of a trait is controlled by two or more genes in which the dominant allele of each gene contributes only a unit fraction of the trait and total phenotypic expression is the sum total of a additive or cumulative effect of all the dominant alleles of genes/polygenes.

Phenylketonuria, also called PKU, is a rare inherited disorder that causes an amino acid called phenylalanine to build up in the body. PKU is caused by a change in the phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) gene. This gene helps create the enzyme needed to break down phenylalanine.

The genes that can give you red-green color blindness are passed down on the X chromosome. Since it's passed down on the X chromosome, red-green color blindness is more common in men. This is because: Males have only 1 X chromosome, from their mother.
SCD is a genetic condition that is present at birth. It is inherited when a child receives two genes—one from each parent—that code for abnormal hemoglobin.

Human skin color is an example of such polygenic inheritance which is controlled by three pairs of polygenes A, B and C. Negro/black color is due to presence of all the six dominant contributing alleles AABBCC. Very light color or white color is due to presence of all six recessive non­-contributing alleles aabbcc.

options.text: Independent assortment principle may be wrong
options.explanation: The principle of independent assortment is not wrong. The idea behind independent assortment is that genes are inherited independently of one another. In other words, the genetic factors that control crest and sex are physically separate. Because the genetic factors are physically separate, they segregate independently during gamete (egg and sperm) formation.

options.text: Mendel might not have studied all the combinations
options.explanation: If in one of the combinations independent assortment is not observed that means Mendel might not have studied all the combinations.
Not observing one of the combinations independently is not impossible.

Mendel's law of independent assortment states that the alleles of two (or more) different genes get sorted into gametes independently of one another. In other words, the allele a gamete receives for one gene does not influence the allele received for another gene.

questions.explanation: Law of independent assortment states that when two individuals differ from each other in two or more pairs of factors, the inheritance of one pair is quite independent of the inheritance of other. Law of independent assortment is applicable to only those factors or genes which are located on different chromosomes.

questions.text: Segregation of Mendelian factors (no linkage, no crossing over) occurs during:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In meiosis, Anaphase I is the stage during which the segregation of Mendelian factors (also known as alleles or genes) occurs. During Anaphase I, the paired homologous chromosomes separate and move towards opposite poles of the cell. This separation is called disjunction. As a result, each daughter cell receives one randomly selected homologous chromosome from each pair. This random assortment of chromosomes ensures the independent segregation of different alleles carried on each chromosome, leading to genetic diversity in the resulting gametes.

Anaphase II is the stage that occurs during the second round of cell division in meiosis, specifically in the process of meiosis II. In this stage, the sister chromatids separate and move towards opposite poles of the cell. However, this stage is not involved in the segregation of Mendelian factors because the sister chromatids are already identical copies of each other. Anaphase II ensures that each daughter cell receives a complete set of chromosomes.

Diplotene is a stage that occurs during prophase I of meiosis. It is characterized by the pairing of homologous chromosomes and the formation of a structure called a bivalent or tetrad. During this stage, genetic recombination or crossing over may occur, where sections of homologous chromosomes exchange genetic material. However, the segregation of Mendelian factors does not occur during diplotene. Instead, diplotene is involved in the exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes.

Metaphase I is the stage that follows prophase I in meiosis. During metaphase I, the bivalents align at the equator of the cell. The homologous chromosomes within each bivalent are paired, but the bivalents themselves are arranged randomly at the metaphase plate. While metaphase I is important for the proper alignment and distribution of homologous chromosomes, it is not the stage where the segregation of Mendelian factors occurs. The segregation of homologous chromosomes occurs during Anaphase I.

In this stage, the sister chromatids separate and move towards opposite poles of the cell. However, this stage is not involved in the segregation of Mendelian factors because the sister chromatids are already identical copies of each other. Anaphase II ensures that each daughter cell receives a complete set of chromosomes. c) Diplotene: Diplotene is a stage that occurs during prophase I of meiosis. It is characterized by the pairing of homologous chromosomes and the formation of a structure called a bivalent or tetrad. During this stage, genetic recombination or crossing over may occur, where sections of homologous chromosomes exchange genetic material. However, the segregation of Mendelian factors does not occur during diplotene. Instead, diplotene is involved in the exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes. d) Metaphase I: Metaphase I is the stage that follows prophase I in meiosis. The homologous chromosomes within each bivalent are paired, but the bivalents themselves are arranged randomly at the metaphase plate. While metaphase I is important for the proper alignment and distribution of homologous chromosomes, it is not the stage where the segregation of Mendelian factors occurs. The segregation of homologous chromosomes occurs during Anaphase I. In summary, the correct option is a) Anaphase I, as it is the stage where the segregation of Mendelian factors takes place.

questions.text: An organism with two identical alleles is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The term "dominant" refers to an allele that is expressed or observed in the phenotype of an organism. In genetics, dominant alleles are represented by uppercase letters, while recessive alleles are represented by lowercase letters. Dominant alleles can mask the effects of recessive alleles. However, having two identical alleles does not necessarily imply dominance. Both dominant and recessive alleles can exist in a homozygous state.

The term "hybrid" is used to describe the offspring of two different species or varieties of organisms. It is not applicable in the context of a single organism having two identical alleles. Hybridization involves the combination of genetic material from different sources, resulting in offspring with a mixture of traits from each parent.

The term "heterozygous" refers to a condition where an organism has two different alleles for a particular gene. In this case, the alleles can be either dominant or recessive. Heterozygotes typically display the phenotype associated with the dominant allele. However, if an organism has two identical alleles, it is not heterozygous because it lacks the presence of different alleles.

The term "homozygous" describes a condition where an organism has two identical alleles for a specific gene. Homozygotes can have either two dominant alleles (referred to as homozygous dominant) or two recessive alleles (referred to as homozygous recessive). In this case, since the question states that the organism has two identical alleles, it is homozygous.

questions.text: A colour blind mother and normal father would have offsprings:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Colour blind sons and normal/carrier daughters
options.explanation: The reason for this is that color blindness is a sex-linked genetic disorder, primarily affecting males. The genes responsible for color vision are located on the X chromosome. Females have two X chromosomes (XX), while males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY). In this scenario, the mother is color blind, which means she has the color blindness gene on one of her X chromosomes. The father, being normal, does not carry the color blindness gene. If the mother passes on her X chromosome with the color blindness gene to a son, he will be color blind because the Y chromosome from the father does not have a corresponding gene to compensate for the color blindness gene on the X chromosome. Daughters, on the other hand, inherit one X chromosome from the mother and one X chromosome from the father. If the daughter receives the X chromosome with the color blindness gene from the mother, she will be a carrier of color blindness but will not typically show symptoms. However, if she receives the normal X chromosome from the father, she will not be color blind. Therefore, the most likely outcome is that the couple will have color blind sons (inheriting the color blindness gene from the mother) and normal or carrier daughters (depending on which X chromosome they receive).

questions.text: Down’s syndrome is due to
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Crossing over is a normal genetic process that occurs during meiosis (cell division in reproductive cells) where genetic material is exchanged between homologous chromosomes. While crossing over can lead to genetic recombination and variation, it is not the cause of Down's syndrome.

Linkage refers to the tendency of genes located close to each other on a chromosome to be inherited together. While linkage can influence the inheritance patterns of certain traits, it is not directly related to the occurrence of Down's syndrome.

Sex-linked inheritance refers to the inheritance of genes located on the sex chromosomes (X and Y chromosomes). Down's syndrome is not caused by a gene on the sex chromosomes, so sex-linked inheritance is not relevant to this condition.

Non-disjunction refers to the failure of chromosomes to separate properly during cell division, resulting in an abnormal distribution of chromosomes in the resulting cells. In the case of Down's syndrome, non-disjunction occurs during the formation of the reproductive cells (eggs or sperm) or during early embryonic development.

questions.explanation: The correct option is d) Non-disjunction of chromosomes. Down's syndrome, also known as trisomy 21, is a genetic disorder caused by the presence of an extra copy of chromosome 21. Non-disjunction refers to the failure of chromosomes to separate properly during cell division, resulting in an abnormal distribution of chromosomes in the resulting cells. In the case of Down's syndrome, non-disjunction occurs during the formation of the reproductive cells (eggs or sperm) or during early embryonic development. Now, let's examine why the other options are incorrect: a) Crossing over: Crossing over is a normal genetic process that occurs during meiosis (cell division in reproductive cells) where genetic material is exchanged between homologous chromosomes. While crossing over can lead to genetic recombination and variation, it is not the cause of Down's syndrome. b) Linkage: Linkage refers to the tendency of genes located close to each other on a chromosome to be inherited together. While linkage can influence the inheritance patterns of certain traits, it is not directly related to the occurrence of Down's syndrome. c) Sex-linked inheritance: Sex-linked inheritance refers to the inheritance of genes located on the sex chromosomes (X and Y chromosomes). Down's syndrome is not caused by a gene on the sex chromosomes, so sex-linked inheritance is not relevant to this condition. In summary, the correct cause of Down's syndrome is non-disjunction of chromosomes, leading to the presence of an extra copy of chromosome 21.

questions.text: In human beings 45 chromosomes/ single X/XO abnormality causes
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Down's syndrome is caused by the presence of an extra copy of chromosome 21. Instead of the usual two copies, individuals with Down's syndrome have three copies of chromosome 21 (47 chromosomes in total), resulting in various physical and intellectual disabilities. This is different from the 45 chromosomes and X/XO abnormality seen in the question.

Klinefelter syndrome is a chromosomal disorder that affects males. It is caused by the presence of at least one extra X chromosome in addition to the usual XY sex chromosomes. Typically, individuals with Klinefelter syndrome have 47 chromosomes (XXY), not 45 chromosomes as stated in the question.

Turner's syndrome is a chromosomal disorder that occurs in females and is characterized by the absence or partial absence of the second sex chromosome (X chromosome). Instead of the usual 46 chromosomes, individuals with Turner's syndrome have 45 chromosomes, with a single X chromosome (45,X) or with other structural abnormalities of the X chromosome.

Edward's syndrome is caused by the presence of an extra copy of chromosome 18. Individuals with Edward's syndrome have three copies of chromosome 18 (47 chromosomes in total), leading to severe developmental abnormalities. This condition is distinct from the 45 chromosomes and X/XO abnormality mentioned in the question.

questions.text: A man of A­ blood group marries a woman of AB blood group. Which type of progeny would indicate that man is heterozygous A?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This blood type indicates that both parents have contributed the A and B alleles. Since the mother donated B allele, the father must have donated A allele. This doesn't show heterozygous nature of the father i.e him donating an O allele.

This blood type can result from inheriting the A allele from either parent. It is possible for the progeny to have blood type A regardless of whether the man is homozygous A (AA) or heterozygous A (AO). Therefore, this option does not indicate that the man is heterozygous A.

Blood type O occurs when neither the A nor B alleles are inherited from either parent. It is not possible to have progeny with blood type O if the woman is of AB blood type as she will pass either A or B to her children.

This blood type can only be inherited if the man is heterozygous A (AO) and passes on the B allele to the progeny. The woman, being AB, will contribute the A allele. Thus, if the progeny has blood type B, it indicates that the man is heterozygous A.

questions.explanation: The correct option would be d): B. To understand why, let's review the basics of blood types and their inheritance. Blood types are determined by the presence or absence of specific antigens (A and B) on the surface of red blood cells. The ABO blood group system includes four main blood types: A, B, AB, and O. In terms of inheritance, the ABO blood types are determined by multiple alleles: A, B, and O. Each person inherits one allele from each parent, resulting in different combinations of blood types. Given that the man has blood type A and the woman has blood type AB, there are two possibilities for the man's genotype: he could be homozygous A (genotype AA) or heterozygous A (genotype AO). To determine which option is correct, we need to look at the possible blood types of their progeny. This blood type can only be inherited if the man is heterozygous A (AO) and passes on the B allele to the progeny. The woman, being AB, will contribute the A allele. Thus, if the progeny has blood type B, it indicates that the man is heterozygous A. In summary, the correct option is d) B because it is the only blood type that indicates the man is heterozygous A (AO). The other options (AB, A, and O) can occur regardless of whether the man is homozygous A or heterozygous A.

questions.text: Multiple alleles control inheritance of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a genetic disorder caused by a mutation in the PAH gene. It is an autosomal recessive disorder, meaning it is controlled by two alleles of the same gene. In this case, individuals with PKU have two copies of the mutated allele. Therefore, PKU is not controlled by multiple alleles but rather by a single mutated allele.

Colour blindness, specifically red-green colour blindness, is a genetic condition caused by a mutation in the genes responsible for the red and green cone cells in the eyes. It is an X-linked recessive disorder. Since it is X-linked, it is controlled by alleles on the X chromosome. However, it is not controlled by multiple alleles but rather by a single mutated allele on the X chromosome.

Sickle cell anaemia is an inherited blood disorder caused by a mutation in the HBB gene, which affects the structure of hemoglobin in red blood cells. It is also an autosomal recessive disorder. However, it is not controlled by multiple alleles but rather by a single mutated allele of the HBB gene.

The ABO blood group system, which includes blood types A, B, AB, and O, is controlled by multiple alleles. The ABO system involves three alleles: A, B, and O. These alleles determine the presence or absence of specific antigens (A or B) on the surface of red blood cells. The inheritance of blood groups follows a codominant pattern, where the A and B alleles are codominant, and the O allele is recessive to both A and B. This allows for different combinations of alleles and different blood types to be expressed.

questions.text: The contrasting pairs of factors in Mendelian crosses are called:

However, multiple alleles do not specifically refer to the contrasting pairs of factors in Mendelian crosses.

In Mendelian genetics, allelomorphs are alternative forms of a gene that occupy the same locus on homologous chromosomes and determine different expressions of a particular trait. They represent the different versions of a gene that give rise to different phenotypes, such as the dominant and recessive forms in a monohybrid cross.
The term "alloloci" is not a commonly used term in genetics. It does not refer to the contrasting pairs of factors in Mendelian crosses.

However, paramorphs are not specifically related to the contrasting pairs of factors in Mendelian crosses.

questions.text: First geneticist/father of genetics was
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hugo de Vries was a Dutch botanist and geneticist who made significant contributions to the understanding of plant genetics. He proposed the theory of "mutationism," suggesting that new species arise through sudden and significant changes in hereditary traits. While de Vries made important contributions to the field of genetics, he is not considered the first geneticist or the father of genetics. That distinction goes to Gregor Mendel.

Gregor Mendel is considered the father of genetics. He was an Austrian monk and botanist who conducted groundbreaking experiments on pea plants in the mid-19th century. Through his meticulous observations and experiments, Mendel established the fundamental principles of inheritance, which laid the foundation for the field of genetics.

Charles Darwin was an English naturalist and biologist known for his theory of evolution through natural selection. While Darwin's work had a profound impact on our understanding of evolution, he did not specifically focus on genetics or establish the field of genetics. His work laid the foundation for the theory of evolution, but Mendel's experiments on inheritance were crucial for understanding the mechanisms of heredity.

Thomas Hunt Morgan was an American geneticist and embryologist who made significant contributions to the field of genetics, particularly in understanding the role of chromosomes in heredity. Morgan conducted groundbreaking experiments on fruit flies and demonstrated the association between specific genes and their locations on chromosomes. While Morgan's work was influential in advancing our understanding of genetics, he is not considered the first geneticist or the father of genetics. That distinction goes to Gregor Mendel.

questions.explanation: The correct option is b) Gregor Mendel. Gregor Mendel is considered as the father of genetics. He was an Austrian monk and botanist who conducted groundbreaking experiments on pea plants in the mid-19th century. Through his meticulous observations and experiments, Mendel established the fundamental principles of inheritance, which laid the foundation for the field of genetics.

questions.text: Mendel’s last law is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mendel's second law, also known as the law of segregation, states that during gamete formation, the two alleles for each gene segregate (separate) from each other so that each gamete carries only one allele for each gene.

Dominance refers to the relationship between different alleles of a gene, where one allele (the dominant allele) masks or suppresses the expression of another allele (the recessive allele). While dominance plays a crucial role in inheritance patterns, it is not Mendel's last law.

Mendel's last law, also known as the third law or the law of independent assortment, states that during the formation of gametes (sex cells), the segregation of alleles for one gene occurs independently of the segregation of alleles for another gene. In other words, the alleles for different traits segregate independently of each other during gamete formation.

Polygenic inheritance refers to the inheritance of traits that are controlled by multiple genes. These traits often show continuous variation and are influenced by the combined effects of multiple genes and environmental factors. Polygenic inheritance is not Mendel's last law, as Mendel primarily focused on the inheritance of traits controlled by a single gene in his experiments with pea plants.

questions.explanation: The correct option is c) Independent Assortment. c)Mendel's last law, also known as the third law or the law of independent assortment, states that during the formation of gametes (sex cells), the segregation of alleles for one gene occurs independently of the segregation of alleles for another gene. In other words, the alleles for different traits segregate independently of each other during gamete formation.

questions.text: Blue eye colour is recessive to brown eye colour. A brown eyed man whose mother was blue eyed marries a blue-­eyed women. The children will be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Both blue eyed and brown eyed 1 : 1
options.explanation: If the brown-eyed man whose mother was blue-eyed marries a blue-eyed woman, their children can inherit either a blue-eye allele from both parents (blue-eyed) or a blue-eye allele from one parent and a brown-eye allele from the other parent (brown-eyed). The expected ratio is 1:1 for blue-eyed and brown-eyed children.

If blue eye color is recessive and brown eye color is dominant, and the brown-eyed man's mother was blue-eyed, it means that the man must have received one copy of the blue-eye allele from his mother and one copy of the brown-eye allele from his father. Since brown eye color is dominant, he will express the brown eye color phenotype. However, if the man marries a blue-eyed woman, who most likely has two copies of the blue-eye allele, all their children cannot be exclusively brown-eyed. Therefore, the option of all brown-eyed children is incorrect.

If blue eye color is recessive and both parents have blue eyes, it means that both parents must have two copies of the blue-eye allele. In this case, all their children are expected to have blue eyes, as they will inherit one blue-eye allele from each parent. However, the given scenario describes a brown-eyed man, which indicates that he has at least one copy of the brown-eye allele. Therefore, the option of all blue-eyed children is incorrect.

In this scenario, if blue eye color is recessive and the brown-eyed man's mother was blue-eyed, it means that the man must have received one copy of the blue-eye allele from his mother and one copy of the brown-eye allele from his father. If he marries a blue-eyed woman who also has two copies of the blue-eye allele, their children can inherit either a blue-eye allele from both parents or a blue-eye allele from one parent and a brown-eye allele from the other parent. This would result in a combination of blue-eyed and brown-eyed children. The expected ratio would be 1:1, rather than 3:1. Therefore, the option of blue-eyed and brown-eyed children in a 3:1 ratio is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct option is Both blue-eyed and brown-eyed, 1:1. In summary, the correct option is Both blue-eyed and brown-eyed, 1:1, because if the brown-eyed man whose mother was blue-eyed marries a blue-eyed woman, their children can inherit either a blue-eye allele from both parents (blue-eyed) or a blue-eye allele from one parent and a brown-eye allele from the other parent (brown-eyed). The expected ratio is 1:1 for blue-eyed and brown-eyed children.

questions.text: The allele which is unable to express its effect in the presence of another is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Codominance refers to a situation where both alleles of a gene are expressed simultaneously and equally in the phenotype of a heterozygous individual. In codominance, neither allele is dominant over the other, and both alleles contribute to the observable traits. This option is incorrect because codominance does not describe an allele that is unable to express its effect in the presence of another allele.

"Supplementary" is not a term commonly used in genetics to describe the relationship between alleles. It does not accurately describe an allele that is unable to express its effect in the presence of another allele.

In genetics, the term "complementary" usually refers to the pairing of nucleotides in DNA, where adenine (A) pairs with thymine (T) and cytosine (C) pairs with guanine (G). It is not used to describe the relationship between alleles in a way that indicates one allele is unable to express its effect in the presence of another.

The term "recessive" accurately describes an allele that is unable to express its effect in the presence of a dominant allele. A recessive allele is only expressed phenotypically when an individual has two copies of the recessive allele (homozygous recessive). In the presence of a dominant allele, the recessive allele is masked and its effect is not observed in the phenotype of heterozygous individuals. This option is correct because it accurately describes the given scenario.

questions.explanation: The correct option is d): Recessive. The term "recessive" accurately describes an allele that is unable to express its effect in the presence of a dominant allele. A recessive allele is only expressed phenotypically when an individual has two copies of the recessive allele (homozygous recessive). In the presence of a dominant allele, the recessive allele is masked and its effect is not observed in the phenotype of heterozygous individuals. This option is correct because it accurately describes the given scenario.

questions.text: Red (RR) Antirrhinum is crossed with white (WW) one. Offspring are pink (RW). This is an example of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In dominance, one allele (dominant allele) is expressed and masks the expression of the other allele (recessive allele) in the heterozygous genotype. In the given scenario, the pink phenotype of the offspring does not represent dominance, as neither the red allele nor the white allele is dominant over the other.

Incomplete dominance occurs when the heterozygous genotype (RW) produces an intermediate phenotype that is a blend or combination of the two homozygous phenotypes (red and white). In this case, the pink color of the offspring is a result of incomplete dominance, where neither the red allele nor the white allele is completely dominant over the other.

Hybrid refers to the offspring resulting from a cross between two different species, varieties, or genotypes. While the cross described involves two different alleles (red and white), it does not necessarily involve different species or varieties. The term "hybrid" does not accurately describe the scenario.

Supplementary genes refer to a type of gene interaction where two or more pairs of genes work together to produce a particular phenotype. The concept of supplementary genes is not applicable to the given scenario, as the pink color in the offspring is not a result of multiple pairs of genes working together. It is instead an example of incomplete dominance, as mentioned earlier.

questions.explanation: The correct option is b) Incomplete dominance. In the given scenario, the cross between a red (RR) Antirrhinum (snapdragon) and a white (WW) one results in pink offspring (RW). This is an example of incomplete dominance, also known as intermediate inheritance. Incomplete dominance occurs when the heterozygous genotype (RW) produces an intermediate phenotype that is a blend or combination of the two homozygous phenotypes (red and white). In this case, the pink color of the offspring is a result of incomplete dominance, where neither the red allele nor the white allele is completely dominant over the other.

questions.text: A colour blind girl is rare because she will be born only when:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Her mother and maternal grand father were colour blind
options.explanation: If the mother and maternal grandfather were color blind, the daughter would have a higher chance of being a carrier of the color blindness allele. However, for the daughter to be color blind, she needs to inherit the recessive allele from her father. This option does not guarantee that the father is color blind, so it is incorrect.

options.text: Her father and maternal grand father were colour blind
options.explanation: A colour blind girl is rare because she will be born only when her father and maternal grand-­father were colour blind. The genotype of the mother was to be either Xc Xc or X c X and that of father Xc Y so that the daughter becomes colour blind.

options.text: Her mother is colour blind and father has normal vision
options.explanation: In this case, if the mother is color blind, she would pass on the recessive color blindness allele to her daughter. However, for the daughter to be color blind, the father must also carry the color blindness allele and pass it on to his daughter. This option does not mention the father's color vision status, so it is not a definitive condition for the daughter to be color blind. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Parents have normal vision but grand parents were colour blind
options.explanation: If the parents have normal vision, it means they do not carry the color blindness allele. In this case, for the daughter to be color blind, both parents must be carriers of the recessive color blindness allele. If the grandparents were color blind, it increases the likelihood that the parents are carriers, but it does not guarantee that the parents themselves have the color blindness allele. Therefore, this option is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct option is b) Her father and maternal grandfather were color blind. Color blindness is a sex-linked recessive disorder that is carried on the X chromosome. The inheritance pattern of color blindness follows an X-linked recessive pattern. In this pattern, a female can be affected by color blindness if she inherits two copies of the recessive allele, one from her father and one from her mother.

questions.text: ABO blood group system is due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Multifactor inheritance refers to traits that are influenced by the interaction of multiple genes and environmental factors. While traits such as height or intelligence can be influenced by multifactor inheritance, the ABO blood group system is primarily determined by multiple alleles rather than the interaction of multiple genes. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

In incomplete dominance, the heterozygous phenotype is an intermediate or blend of the two homozygous phenotypes. However, the ABO blood group system does not exhibit incomplete dominance. Instead, it follows a pattern of codominance, where both the A and B alleles are expressed simultaneously and equally when present in the heterozygous genotype (AB). Therefore, this option is incorrect.

The ABO blood group system is determined by multiple allelism. The ABO system involves three alleles: A, B, and O. These alleles determine the presence or absence of specific antigens (A or B) on the surface of red blood cells. Each individual inherits two alleles, one from each parent, resulting in different combinations of alleles and different blood types.

Epistasis refers to the interaction between different genes where one gene masks or modifies the expression of another gene. The ABO blood group system is not an example of epistasis, as the different blood types are determined by the presence or absence of specific antigens controlled by multiple alleles (A, B, and O) rather than the interaction of different genes. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct option is c) Multiple allelism. The ABO blood group system is determined by multiple allelism. The ABO system involves three alleles: A, B, and O. These alleles determine the presence or absence of specific antigens (A or B) on the surface of red blood cells. Each individual inherits two alleles, one from each parent, resulting in different combinations of alleles and different blood types.

questions.text: In Down’s syndrome of a male child, the sex complement is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: XO represents Turner syndrome, which is a condition that affects females. In Turner syndrome, females have only one X chromosome instead of the usual two. This condition is characterized by various physical and developmental features, but it is not associated with Down syndrome.

In Down’s syndrome of a male child, the sex complement is XY. Down syndrome is an autosomal birth defect caused by the presence of an extra chromosome number 21 (three instead of two number 21 chromosomes or trisomy 21).

XX represents the typical sex complement of a female. In Down syndrome, regardless of the gender, there is an extra copy of chromosome 21, which does not affect the sex chromosomes. Therefore, the sex complement remains XY for males and XX for females.

XXY represents Klinefelter syndrome, which is a condition that affects males. In Klinefelter syndrome, males have an extra X chromosome, resulting in a karyotype of 47,XXY. Although Klinefelter syndrome involves an extra chromosome, it is not related to Down syndrome.

questions.explanation: The correct option is b) XY. Down syndrome: also known as trisomy 21, is a genetic disorder caused by the presence of an extra copy of chromosome 21. It typically occurs due to nondisjunction during the formation of reproductive cells.

questions.text: Which one is a hereditary disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While cataracts can be caused by various factors, including aging, trauma, or certain medical conditions, they are not primarily considered a hereditary disease. Cataracts can occur due to a combination of genetic and environmental factors, but they are not typically inherited in a straightforward genetic manner.

Leprosy, also known as Hansen's disease, is an infectious disease caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium leprae. It is not considered a hereditary disease as it is transmitted through close and prolonged contact with infected individuals. Leprosy is not primarily caused by genetic factors but rather by exposure to the bacterium.

Blindness itself is not a specific hereditary disease but rather a condition that can result from various causes, including genetic factors, congenital conditions, injuries, infections, or other medical conditions. While there are genetic disorders that can cause blindness, blindness as a general concept cannot be considered a hereditary disease.

Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a hereditary disease caused by a genetic mutation that affects the metabolism of the amino acid phenylalanine. It is an autosomal recessive disorder, meaning that both copies of the gene must be mutated for an individual to have the condition. PKU is inherited from the parents who carry the mutated gene.

questions.explanation: The correct option is d) Phenylketonuria. Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a hereditary disease caused by a genetic mutation that affects the metabolism of the amino acid phenylalanine. It is an autosomal recessive disorder, meaning that both copies of the gene must be mutated for an individual to have the condition. PKU is inherited from the parents who carry the mutated gene.

questions.text: Bateson used the terms coupling and repulsion for linkage and crossing over. Name the correct parental of coupling type alongwith its cross over or repulsion.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Coupling AABB, aabb; Repulsion AABB, aabb
options.explanation: Bateson and Punnet explained that when two dominants enter from the same parent­ they try to remain together, called coupling. When two dominants enter from different parents they try to remain seperate called repulsion. Bateson and Punnett (1906) used the term coupling and repulsion in sweet pea (Lathyrus odoratus) for linkage and crossing over. The correct parental of coupling type along with its cross over or repulsion is coupling AABB, aabb : Repulsion AAbb; aaBB.

questions.text: A normal green male maize is crossed with albino female. The progeny is albino because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Trait for a albinism is dominant
options.explanation: If the trait for albinism were dominant, it would mean that a single copy of the dominant allele would be sufficient to express the albino phenotype. In this case, if the male parent is normal green (homozygous dominant), the progeny would not be albino. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: The albinos have a biochemical to destroy plastids derived from green male
options.explanation: The albinos have a biochemical to destroy plastids derived from the green male: This option suggests that albinos have a biochemical mechanism to destroy plastids derived from the green male. However, the color of maize is primarily determined by the presence or absence of chloroplasts, which contain chlorophyll responsible for the green color. The absence of chloroplasts or impaired chlorophyll synthesis leads to albinism. It is not related to a biochemical mechanism to destroy plastids derived from the male parent. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Plastids are inherited from female parent
options.explanation: Plastids are inherited from the female parent: The correct option is c) Plastids are inherited from the female parent. In plants, plastids, including chloroplasts, are typically inherited maternally. This means that the progeny receive plastids from the female parent. In the given cross, if the female parent is albino and does not have functional chloroplasts, the progeny will also lack chloroplasts and appear albino. Therefore, this option is correct.

options.text: Green plastids of male must have mutated
options.explanation: Green plastids of the male must have mutated: This option suggests that the green plastids of the male parent must have undergone a mutation. However, in this scenario, the color of the progeny is determined by the presence or absence of functional chloroplasts, not by a mutation in the plastids of the male parent. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct option is c) Plastids are inherited from the female parent. In plants, plastids, including chloroplasts, are typically inherited maternally. questions.text: In Mendel's experiments, the phenotypic ratio of recessive to dominant plants is equal to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mendel actually observed 3 : 1 phenotypic ratio in F2. His phenotypic data of F3 canalso be explained on the basis of 1 : 2 : 1 genotypic ratio of F2.
Mendel actually observed 3 : 1 phenotypic ratio in F2. His phenotypic data of F3 canalso be explained on the basis of 1 : 2 : 1 genotypic ratio of F2.

In Mendel's experiments, the phenotypic ratio of recessive to dominant plants was 3:1. Mendel conducted experiments on pea plants with two contrasting traits, such as seed color (yellow versus green) and flower color (purple versus white). He crossed two pure-breeding parent plants, one with yellow seeds and one with green seeds. All of the offspring of the first generation (F1) were yellow. This was puzzling because Mendel knew that both parents had the genes for both yellow and green seed color. He hypothesized that only one of the genes was expressed in the F1 offspring and that the other gene was hidden. He called the expressed gene the dominant gene, and the hidden gene the recessive gene. To test his hypothesis, Mendel crossed two F1 offspring together. The offspring of this second generation (F2) showed a 3:1 phenotypic ratio of yellow: and green seeds. This meant that 75% of the offspring had the dominant yellow phenotype, and 25% had the recessive green phenotype. Mendel's experiments demonstrated that the phenotypic ratio of recessive to dominant traits is always 3:1. This is because each organism inherits two copies of each gene, one from each parent. If the dominant gene is present on both copies of a gene, the organism will have the dominant phenotype. However, if the recessive gene is present on both copies of a gene, the organism will have the recessive phenotype.
Mendel actually observed 3 : 1 phenotypic ratio in F2. His phenotypic data of F3 canalso be explained on the basis of 1 : 2 : 1 genotypic ratio of F2.

options.explanation: Alleles do not arise by genetic drift. Genetic drift is a random change in the frequency of alleles in a population. It can cause the loss of rare alleles and the fixation of common alleles. However, it cannot create new alleles. Genetic drift is a powerful force in evolution, but it cannot create new alleles. Only mutation can do that.

Alleles do not arise by natural selection. Natural selection is a process that acts on alleles that already exist in a population. It does not create new alleles. Natural selection can also act on neutral mutations, meaning that they have no effect on the organism's survival or reproduction. Neutral mutations are neither beneficial nor harmful, and they are passed on to the next generation at the same rate as the other alleles in the population. The combination of mutation and natural selection is what drives evolution. Over time, natural selection can lead to changes in the frequency of alleles in a population, which can lead to the evolution of new traits and adaptations.

This can change the way the protein functions, which can lead to a change in the organism's phenotype. Mutations can be either harmful, beneficial, or neutral. Harmful mutations can cause disease or reduce the organism's fitness. Beneficial mutations can give the organism a competitive advantage. Neutral mutations do not have any effect on the organism's phenotype.

The B allele is responsible for the production of the B antigen in red blood cells. The O allele is responsible for the production of neither antigen on red blood cells. A person's blood type is determined by the alleles they inherit from their parents. If a person inherits two A alleles, they will have type A blood. If a person inherits two B alleles, they will have type B blood. If a person inherits one A allele and one B allele, they will have type AB blood. And if a person inherits two O alleles, they will have type O blood. Multiple alleles are a common occurrence in human genetics. Other examples of multiple alleles include the Rh blood group system, the MN blood group system, and the sickle cell anemia gene.

The Rh factor can be responsible for a condition called erythroblastosis fetalis. Erythroblastosis fetalis, also known as hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN), occurs when there is an incompatibility between the blood types of the mother and the fetus, specifically related to the Rh factor. Hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN) is also known as alloimmune HDFN or erythroblastosis fetalis. It is caused by the destruction of neonatal red blood cells by maternal immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibodies. The formation of maternal antibodies in response to a fetal antigen is called isoimmunization.

questions.explanation: Yes, the Rh factor can be responsible for a condition called erythroblastosis fetalis. Erythroblastosis fetalis, also known as hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN), occurs when there is an incompatibility between the blood types of the mother and the fetus, specifically related to the Rh factor. The Rh factor refers to a specific antigen found on the surface of red blood cells. If a mother is Rh-negative (lacks the Rh factor) and the fetus is Rh-positive (has the Rh factor), the mother's immune system may produce antibodies against the Rh factor if exposed to it during pregnancy or childbirth. In subsequent pregnancies with Rh-positive fetuses, these maternal antibodies can cross the placenta and attack the fetal red blood cells, leading to the destruction of red blood cells and causing erythroblastosis fetalis. This condition can result in severe anemia, jaundice, organ damage, and, in severe cases, fetal or newborn death. Rh factor incompatibility can be prevented and managed through Rh immunoglobulin (RhIg) administration to Rh-negative mothers during pregnancy and childbirth, which prevents the formation of Rh antibodies and reduces the risk of erythroblastosis fetalis.

Autosomal dominant is a pattern of inheritance characteristic of some genetic disorders. “Autosomal” means that the gene in question is located on one of the numbered, or non-sex, chromosomes. “Dominant” means that a single copy of the mutated gene (from one parent) is enough to cause the disorder.

Families with an X-linked dominant disorder often have both affected males and affected females in each generation. A striking characteristic of X-linked inheritance is that fathers cannot pass X-linked traits to their sons; fathers only pass X chromosomes to their daughters and Y chromosomes to their sons.

Autosomal recessive is a pattern of inheritance characteristic of some genetic disorders. “Autosomal” means that the gene in question is located on one of the numbered, or non-sex, chromosomes. “Recessive” means that two copies of the mutated gene (one from each parent) are required to cause the disorder.

Dominant refers to the relationship between two versions of a gene. Individuals receive two versions of each gene, known as alleles, from each parent. Epistasis is a circumstance where the expression of one gene is modified (e.g., masked, inhibited or suppressed) by the expression of one or more other genes.

questions.text: The basic unit of biological information is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A gene is a segment of DNA that contains the instructions for building specific molecules, such as proteins. Genes are the basic units of heredity and determine the traits and characteristics of an organism. They play a crucial role in the transmission of genetic information from one generation to the next.
A locus refers to the specific physical location of a gene or a DNA sequence on a chromosome. It is the position of a gene within a genome. Each gene has a unique locus on a chromosome.

A chromosome is a structure composed of DNA and proteins found in the nucleus of cells. Chromosomes carry genetic information and are organized into distinct units that contain genes. Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes, including one pair of sex chromosomes.

An allele is one of the different versions of a gene that can exist at a specific locus on a chromosome. Alleles may vary in their DNA sequence, and they can result in different traits or phenotypes when present in an individual's genome.

questions.explanation: The correct answer to the question is A) Gene. Genes are the fundamental units of biological information that determine an organism's traits and characteristics.

questions.text: A true-breeding variety upon self-fertilization always produces:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In genetics, the term "dominant" refers to the allele that is expressed or observed in the phenotype when an individual has two different alleles for a particular gene. If a true-breeding variety always produces only dominant offspring, it means that all the offspring will express the dominant trait, regardless of the trait's inheritance pattern.

In genetics, the term "recessive" refers to the allele that is only expressed in the phenotype when an individual has two copies of the same recessive allele. If a true-breeding variety always produces only recessive offspring, it means that all the offspring will exhibit the recessive trait, and there will be no individuals expressing the dominant trait.

This option suggests that the true-breeding variety produces both dominant and recessive offspring in a specific ratio of 3 dominant to 1 recessive. This ratio corresponds to the typical Mendelian inheritance pattern observed in a monohybrid cross between two heterozygous individuals.

If a true-breeding variety always produces offspring identical to the parents, it means that the parents are homozygous for a specific trait, and their offspring will also be homozygous for that trait. In this case, the offspring will be genetically identical to the parents.

questions.explanation: The correct option is D) Offspring identical to the parents. True-breeding varieties are homozygous for a particular trait, meaning they carry two identical alleles for that trait. When self-fertilization occurs, the offspring will inherit the same alleles from the parents, resulting in offspring that are genetically identical to the parents.

questions.text: Gene linkage is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gene linkage refers to the tendency of genes located close to each other on the same chromosome to be inherited together as a unit during meiosis. Genes that are physically close on a chromosome have a higher chance of being inherited together, and their linkage is referred to as physical linkage.
Physiological relation is not a term commonly used in genetics or gene linkage. It is not directly related to the concept of gene linkage.

This option suggests that gene linkage involves both physical and physiological relations. However, as explained above, physiological relation is not a commonly used term in the context of gene linkage. The primary relation in gene linkage is physical, referring to the proximity of genes on the same chromosome.
This option indicates that neither physical nor physiological relations are associated with gene linkage.

questions.explanation: The correct option is A) Physical relation of genes. Gene linkage is based on the physical proximity of genes on the same chromosome, leading to the likelihood of them being inherited together.

questions.text: This structure is common in all vertebrates at embryonic stages:

options.explanation: Gill pouches or gill slits are a common feature in all vertebrates during their embryonic stages. They are openings in the pharyngeal region that lead from the pharynx to the outside and are initially developed to support aquatic respiration. questions.text: Variation may be due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mutation is a random change in the DNA sequence of an organism's genome. It is a fundamental source of genetic variation as it introduces new alleles or traits into the gene pool.

Gametogenesis is the process of forming gametes (sex cells) such as sperm and eggs. During this process, genetic recombination can occur through crossing over and independent assortment of chromosomes, leading to new combinations of alleles in the offspring.
Gene flow refers to the transfer of genes between populations through migration and interbreeding, and it also contributes to genetic variation.

All of the options (mutation, genetic recombinant during gametogenesis, genetic drift, and gene flow) contribute to genetic variation in populations. options.text: A reduction of blood clotting factors
options.explanation: This is a valid reason for hemophiliac blood failing to clot properly. questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sex-linked, as related to genetics, refers to characteristics (or traits) that are influenced by genes carried on the sex chromosomes. In humans, the term often refers to traits or disorders influenced by genes on the X chromosome, as it contains many more genes than the smaller Y chromosome

Sex-limited traits are determined by genes located on autosomes and express only in one sex. While these traits are responsible considerably for sexual dimorphism, sex-influenced traits do not show distinctive expression between women and men. Numerous analytical models have treated sex as an essential factor.

Sex-influenced traits are autosomal traits that are influenced by sex. If a male has one recessive allele, he will show that trait, but it will take two recessive for the female to show that same trait. One such gene is baldness. A lot of sex-limtied traits can determine parental carriers by using a pedigree.

In humans, the alleles for certain conditions (including some forms of color blindness, hemophilia, and muscular dystrophy) are X-linked. These diseases are much more common in men than they are in women due to their X-linked inheritance pattern.

questions.text: A trait that can occur in both males and female but is more common in one sex, cannot be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: X-linked recessive trait: This trait refers to a trait that is carried on the X chromosome and is recessive. It means that males, who have one X and one Y chromosome, are more likely to express the trait if they inherit the recessive allele. Females, on the other hand, have two X chromosomes and would need to inherit the recessive allele from both parents to express the trait. Since the trait is more common in one sex (males), this option could be a valid answer.

Sex-influenced trait: Sex-influenced traits are influenced by the sex of an individual but are not limited to one sex. These traits are determined by autosomal genes but can be expressed differently based on the presence of certain sex hormones. For example, pattern baldness is more common in males and is influenced by the presence of testosterone. However, it can also occur in females, albeit less frequently. Therefore, a trait that is more common in one sex but can still occur in the other sex can be a sex-influenced trait. Hence, this option could be incorrect.

Sex-limited trait: A sex-limited trait is a trait that is present exclusively in one sex and cannot occur in the other. For example, pregnancy and breastfeeding are sex-limited traits that can only occur in females. Since the given trait can occur in both males and females, this option is the correct answer.

X-linked dominant trait: This trait refers to a trait carried on the X chromosome that is dominant. In this case, if an individual inherits the dominant allele on one of their X chromosomes, they will express the trait. Since the given trait is more common in one sex but can occur in both sexes, it is unlikely to be an X-linked dominant trait. Therefore, this option could be incorrect.

questions.text: Gregor John Mendel laid down the foundation of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Modern genetics began with the work of the Augustinian friar Gregor Johann Mendel. His work on pea plants, published in 1866, provided the initial evidence that, on its rediscovery in 1900, helped to establish the theory of Mendelian inheritance.

Classical genetics has its origin in Gregor Mendel's papers on inheritance in peas, published in 1866. The chromosome theory of inheritance states that genes are located on chromosomes and that chromosomal dynamics underlie the patterns of Mendelian inheritance.

Population genetics was a vital ingredient in the emergence of the modern evolutionary synthesis. Its primary founders were Sewall Wright, J. B. S. Haldane and Ronald Fisher, who also laid the foundations for the related discipline of quantitative genetics.

questions.explanation: As a result of his monohybrid cross experiments, Gregor Mendel observed the following: Law of Segregation: Mendel found that the offspring of his monohybrid crosses exhibited a 3:1 phenotypic ratio in the first generation (F1). This ratio indicated that one trait was dominant and the other was recessive. The dominant trait appeared in the majority (3/4) of the offspring, while the recessive trait appeared in the minority (1/4) of the offspring. Genotypic Ratios: Mendel also observed a 1:2:1 genotypic ratio in the second generation (F2) of his monohybrid crosses. This ratio indicated that the offspring inherited one allele from each parent, with three possible genotypes: homozygous dominant (AA), heterozygous (Aa), and homozygous recessive (aa). Principle of Independent Assortment: In addition to the monohybrid crosses, Mendel performed dihybrid crosses to study the inheritance of two different traits. He observed that the alleles for different traits segregated independently during gamete formation, demonstrating the principle of independent assortment. Overall, Mendel's monohybrid crosses provided evidence for the existence of discrete heritable units (genes), the segregation of alleles during gamete formation, and the presence of dominant and recessive traits. His findings laid the foundation for the principles of inheritance and became the basis for modern genetics. An explanation is given below:

questions.text: Genes for color blindness, hemophilia and gout form a linkage group on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A sex chromosome is one of the two types of chromosomes that determine an individual's sex. In humans, there are two sex chromosomes: X and Y. Males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY), while females have two X chromosomes (XX). Genes related to sex-linked traits, such as color blindness and hemophilia, are found on the sex chromosomes.
Autosomes are the non-sex chromosomes, meaning they are the chromosomes that are not involved in determining an individual's sex. In humans, there are 22 pairs of autosomes.

The X-chromosome is one of the two sex chromosomes, and it is found in both males (one X chromosome) and females (two X chromosomes). Traits that are carried on the X-chromosome are called X-linked traits. Color blindness, gout and hemophilia are examples of X-linked traits, meaning the genes responsible for these conditions are located on the X-chromosome.

The Y-chromosome is the other sex chromosome and is found only in males. It plays a crucial role in determining male sex characteristics during development. Since females do not have a Y-chromosome, the genes related to color blindness, hemophilia, or gout are not located on the Y-chromosome.

questions.explanation: Man has 23 linkage groups. Genes for colour blindness, haemophilia, gout etc form one linkage group on human X - chromosome.

questions.explanation: Gene linkage refers to the phenomenon where two or more genes are located close to each other on the same chromosome and tend to be inherited together as a unit. In other words, linked genes are physically close to each other on the same chromosome, and they are usually inherited as a package rather than independently assorting during meiosis. All genes located on the same chromosomes are linked to each other. This phenomenon of staying together is called linkage.

questions.text: Linked genes can be separated by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Genes that are located on the same chromosome are called linked genes. Alleles for these genes tend to segregate together during meiosis, unless they are separated by crossing-over.

Crossing over is an exchange of segments between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes. Linked genes can be separated by crossing over. The farther apart two genes lie, greater are chances of their separation through crossing over.

Linked genes can not be separated by mitosis. Mitosis is the process by which a cell replicates its chromosomes and then segregates them, producing two identical nuclei in preparation for cell division.
Linked genes can not be separated by gametogenesis. Gametogenesis is the production of gametes from haploid precursor cells.

questions.explanation: Linked genes can be separated by crossing over. Closer the gene located, more strongly are their genes linked. The farther apart two genes lie, greater are chances of their separation through crossing over. Linked genes can be separated by a process called genetic recombination, which occurs during the formation of gametes (sperm and egg cells) in meiosis. Genetic recombination is the exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes, leading to new combinations of alleles on the chromosomes.

questions.explanation: The ABO blood group locus is located on chromosome number 9. This locus determines the ABO blood group system, which classifies human blood into four main types: A, B, AB, and O. The ABO blood group system is based on the presence or absence of specific antigens (A and B antigens) on the surface of red blood cells.

questions.explanation: The phenomenon of gene interaction in which a gene interferes with or modifies the expression of another gene is called "epistasis." Epistasis occurs when the effect of one gene (called the "epistatic gene") masks or alters the effect of another gene (called the "hypostatic gene"). This interaction between genes can lead to complex patterns of inheritance and phenotypic outcomes that might not be predictable solely based on the individual gene's effects. Epistasis is a significant factor in understanding the diversity of traits and genetic variations observed in populations.

questions.explanation: Epistasis is an interaction between different genes at different loci (specific positions on a chromosome). It occurs when the expression of one gene affects the expression of another gene, often at a different locus, leading to a modified or masked phenotype. In epistasis, one gene is said to be epistatic, while the gene whose effect is modified or masked is termed hypostatic. Epistasis can result in various patterns of inheritance and contribute to the complexity of genetic traits observed in populations.

questions.text: MN blood group system is an example of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Complete dominance is a condition wherein the allele regarded as dominant completely masks the effect of the allele that is recessive. This is the opposite of codominance. For example, people with blood type A have antigen A. This allele is designated IA.

Incomplete dominance is a form of Gene interaction in which both alleles of a gene at a locus are partially expressed, often resulting in an intermediate or different phenotype. If a white and black dog produce a gray offspring, this is an example of incomplete dominance. Overdominance is a phenomenon in genetics where the phenotype of the heterozygote lies outside the phenotypical range of both homozygous parents.
Overdominance is a phenomenon in genetics where the phenotype of the heterozygote lies outside the phenotypical range of both homozygous parents. e.g. human sickle cell anemia.
Codominance is a heterozygous condition in which both alleles at a gene locus are fully expressed in the phenotype. e.g. MN blood group system.

If heterozygous condition shows greater phenotypic expression than either of the corresponding homozygotes then it is called over dominance.
If two contrasting traits are completely expressed in heterozygous condition, then it is called co dominance.
If two contrasting traits are expressed as a blend in heterozygous condition, then it is called incomplete dominance.

questions.explanation: If two contrasting traits are completely expressed in heterozygous condition, then it is called co dominance.

questions.text: Breakdown product of haemoglobin is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bilirubin is a yellow-orange pigment that is produced during the breakdown of hemoglobin, the protein responsible for carrying oxygen in red blood cells.

Although iron is an essential component of hemoglobin, it is not considered a breakdown product of hemoglobin. Iron is recycled and reused in the body after the breakdown of hemoglobin, but it is not directly released as a breakdown product.

Biliverdin is an intermediate compound formed during the breakdown of heme in hemoglobin. Biliverdin is subsequently converted to bilirubin. Therefore, while biliverdin is involved in the breakdown process, it is not the final breakdown product of hemoglobin.
The correct option is not "Both A and B" because iron is not a breakdown product of hemoglobin. Only bilirubin (option A) is the breakdown product.

questions.explanation: Bilirubin is a yellow-orange pigment that is produced during the breakdown of hemoglobin, the protein responsible for carrying oxygen in red blood cells. When red blood cells reach the end of their lifespan, they are broken down in the spleen and liver. The heme component of hemoglobin is converted to biliverdin, which is then converted to bilirubin. Bilirubin is released into the bloodstream and eventually processed by the liver and excreted in the bile.

questions.text: Child death may occur in the marriage of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In this case, both the man and the woman have the Rh factor, so there is no Rh incompatibility issue. Typically, there is no increased risk of child death due to Rh incompatibility in this scenario. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Maternal-foetal Rh incompatibility Maternal-foetal incompatibility results when an Rh- woman, married to an Rh+ man conceives a child who is Rh+.

Both partners are Rh-negative, which means there is no Rh incompatibility issue. Therefore, the risk of child death due to Rh incompatibility is not applicable in this scenario. Hence, this option is incorrect.

If the Rh-man's sperm carries the Rh antigen, there is a risk of Rh sensitization in the Rh+ woman during pregnancy. Similar to the Rh+ man and Rh-woman scenario, complications can arise, including the possibility of fetal death. However, with proper medical care, these complications can often be prevented or managed effectively.

questions.explanation: Rh factor plays a crucial role in a child’s birth born out of a marriage between Rh- woman and a Rh+ man. In such a case, the mother becomes sensitive while carrying a first Rh+ child within her womb. The reason for such sensitivity is that some of the RBCs from the developing embryo get into the bloodstream of the mother during development, causing her to produce anti­Rh antibodies. In fact, the first child of such parents is nearly normal and is delivered safely. However, if such a mother gets pregnant again, the subsequent Rh fetuses will be exposed to the anti­ Rh antibodies produced by the mother. As a result serious damage to the red blood cells of the developing embryo will occur causing hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN) or erythroblastosis Foetalis. This disease leads to the death of the developing embryo before birth or after parturition.

questions.text: Person with blood group AB is considered as universal recipient because he has:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Both A and B antigens on RBC but no antibodies in the plasma
options.explanation: A person with blood group AB has both A and B antigens on the surface of their red blood cells (RBCs). Antigens are substances that can trigger an immune response in the body. In this case, since the person has both A and B antigens, their immune system does not produce antibodies against these antigens. As a result, when receiving blood transfusions, the person's plasma will not contain antibodies that would attack and destroy the transfused blood cells. Therefore, a person with blood group AB is considered a universal recipient because they can receive blood from individuals with blood groups A, B, AB, and O without an immune response.

options.text: Both A and B antibodies in the plasma
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. If a person has both A and B antibodies in their plasma, it means they have blood group O. Blood group O individuals have neither A nor B antigens on their RBCs, but they have antibodies against both A and B antigens in their plasma. Therefore, they cannot receive blood from individuals with blood groups A, B, or AB.

options.text: No antigen on RBC and no antibody in the plasma
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. If a person has no antigens on their RBCs and no antibodies in their plasma, it means they have blood group O. Blood group O individuals have no antigens on their RBCs, but they have antibodies against both A and B antigens in their plasma. Therefore, they cannot receive blood from individuals with blood groups A, B, or AB.

options.text: Both A and B antigens in the plasma but no antibodies.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Antigens are present on the surface of RBCs, not in the plasma. Having A and B antigens in the plasma is not a recognized blood group classification. The presence of antigens on RBCs and antibodies in the plasma determines a person's blood group compatibility.

questions.explanation: A person with blood group AB has both A and B antigens on the surface of their red blood cells (RBCs). Antigens are substances that can trigger an immune response in the body. In this case, since the person has both A and B antigens, their immune system does not produce antibodies against these antigens. As a result, when receiving blood transfusions, the person's plasma will not contain antibodies that would attack and destroy the transfused blood cells. Therefore, a person with blood group AB is considered a universal recipient because they can receive blood from individuals with blood groups A, B, AB, and O without an immune response.
